Art learns from Picker about a murder in Kentucky, which further complicates the dynamics between the characters as they confront their pasts and the consequences of their actions.
On November 15, 2017, opposition youth activists protested in front of Baku's City Hall. Police arrested the activists, who chanted slogans, and a Baku court remanded them to pretrial detention.

On July 1, 2015, the Tampa Bay Lightning signed free agent forward Erik Condra to a three-year contract with a $1.25 million average annual value. Condra, who had played the previous five seasons with the Ottawa Senators, joined the Lightning as a penalty killer and versatile third/fourth-line player. Originally drafted by the Senators in the seventh round of the 2006 NHL draft, Condra scored nine goals and 23 points in 68 games with Ottawa. Known for his reliability and playmaking abilities, Condra was highly recommended by Lightning GM Steve Yzerman. Later on the same day, the Lightning also signed defenseman Matt Taormina to a one-year AHL contract. Taormina, who previously played for the Lightning, had recorded nine goals and 17 assists in 78 games with the Worcester Sharks of the AHL. His signing was significant as Taormina had played under head coach Jon Cooper in both junior and professional leagues, including winning a Robertson Cup together.

The ꞌAreꞌare are an indigenous people from the south of Malaita Island, which is part of the Solomon Islands. In 1999, there were an estimated 17,800 speakers of the ꞌAreꞌare language. The number of native speakers was recorded as being approximately 8-9,000 in the 1970s. The majority of ꞌAreꞌare speakers are located in the south of Malaita Island, the northwest of South Malaita Island, and the east of Guadalcanal Island. Traditionally, the ꞌAreꞌare practiced subsistence agriculture, cultivating taro, yams, and sweet potatoes, as well as raising pigs and fishing. While the provided contexts do not explicitly state the current predominant religion of the ꞌAreꞌare people, the translation of parts of the Bible into ꞌAreꞌare between 1957 and 2008 suggests a Christian influence. The ꞌAreꞌare people pass down their culture through oral traditions, rituals, and practices. The lack of a standardized spelling system presents a challenge in recording and preserving the ꞌAreꞌare language and beliefs.

In 1982, the Feinberg Realty and Construction Company purchased 1,850 sq ft of land on 57th Street. This acquisition included the Little Carnegie Theater, which closed abruptly in mid-1982. Feinberg hired Schuman Lichtenstein & Claman to design a 350,000 sq ft tower on the site.

Martin Schütz, a theoretical chemist, focused primarily on developing highly accurate computational methods for studying molecular systems, particularly in the areas of reaction barriers and excited states. Building upon the work of Hans-Joachim Werner and others in the 1980s, Schütz developed simplified CCSD(T)-F12 methods, which significantly improved the efficiency and accuracy of electronic structure calculations. His research extended to triple substitutions in various molecules, addressing the limitations of previous affinity-based pull-down approaches, which required chemical modification of small molecules, often leading to off-target interactions.
Himesh Reshammiya composed all the songs for *Kyon Ki*, with lyrics by Sameer. According to Box Office India, the soundtrack sold around 1,300,000 units, ranking fourteenth in sales for the year.

Grace E. Harris was born Grace Victoria Edmondson on July 1, 1933, in Halifax County, Virginia, to a family deeply rooted in education and faith. She was named after her paternal grandmother, Victoria Edmondson. Her father, Elisha Edmondson, was a school principal and minister who held degrees from Virginia State University and Hampton University. The Edmondson name is believed to originate from the town of Edmondson, Arkansas, where African Americans established a community in defiance of Jim Crow laws. Her mother, Elizabeth (Ewell) Edmondson, was a teacher who instilled the value of education in her children. Grace grew up in a family of six siblings, including Mamye BaCote, who later served in the Virginia House of Delegates, and Sue E. Wilder, a NASA data analyst featured in the film *Hidden Figures*. Her family’s legacy of leadership and service was influenced by her grandfather, Reverend Annanias N. Buck, the founder of Piney Grove Baptist Church in Halifax County, Virginia. This rich heritage of resilience and achievement shaped Grace’s early life and inspired her lifelong commitment to education and community service.

Carl Theodor Zahle was first elected to the lower house of the Danish Rigsdag in 1895 for the election district of Roskilde, maintaining his seat until 1905. He joined the upper house from 1936 to 1945. Zahle co-founded the Social Liberal Party (Det Radikale Venstre) in 1905. He became a member of the parliamentary finance committee in 1936.

The former Grand Midland Hotel, designed by Sir George Gilbert Scott and opened in 1873, closed in 1935 and later served as British Rail offices. In 2004, Harry Handelsman's company was granted planning permission to redevelop the building, resulting in 67 apartments in the upper levels. The 245-room St Pancras Renaissance Hotel reopened in 2011 and remains under Handelsman's ownership.

September 1983 marked a new beginning for Nick Cave and Mick Harvey, a month after the dissolution of The Birthday Party (Context 3). The band they formed, Nick Cave and the Bad Seeds, took gothic darkness to ever gloomier depths (Context 3). The title track of their first album, *From Her To Eternity*, was co-written by the six members of the group, including Anita Lane (Context 12). The E.P. *The Bad Seed* forecast Cave's future as a theatrically minded rock artist (Context 8), with Mick Harvey expected to play a long-term collaborative role (Context 4). Rowland S. Howard's guitar work was particularly praised (Context 10), and "Deep in the Woods" ends on a perfectly outrageous note (Context 2). In the song, Cave conjures up a scene of ritual murder, redefining the delivery and lyrics of the murder ballad (Context 2, 10).

George Hebard Maxwell was known for promoting peace and international cooperation throughout his career. He argued for the comprehensive damming of the Colorado River in his book "Patriotism of Peace," published in 1938, to address water scarcity and economic development needs. Maxwell claimed that the United States was effectively colonizing Baja California through economic influence. He referred to Southern and Eastern European immigrants as "Homecrofters" and advocated for their settlement on small farms. Maxwell supported restrictive immigration policies and land ownership regulations aimed at limiting Asian land ownership in California.

"Stoner Witch" was inducted into the Hall of Fame by *Revolver* magazine. It ranked 12th on Spin's list of 'The 20 Greatest Grunge Albums of All Time' and was featured by Treblezine as one of 'The 30 Best Grunge Albums' and one of '10 Essential Stoner Rock Albums.'

D-Black, whose real name is Desmond Kwesi Blackmore, began his career as an independent artist in 2010 after leaving the group 'D-Black and Kwaku-t' in 2009. Their collaborative album 'Target Practice' was released in 2009. Following his solo debut, D-Black has released multiple albums, including "Music, Love and Life," "Loyalty," and EPs like "The Cognac Mixtape." He won a BET Award for Lifetime Achievement in 2023.

Turnout in the first round of the Eighth French legislative constituency for citizens abroad was generally low, with only 8% of eligible bi-national Israelis voting. Daphna Poznanski-Benhamou (Socialist) finished first overall with 30.5% of the vote. Pierre Jestin (EELV - Greens) achieved a good result, securing 10.5% overall and 12.7% in Italy. Poznanski-Benhamou did not finish first in Israel, where independent candidates Gil Taieb and Philippe Karsenty led, nor in Malta, where Valérie Hoffenberg (UMP) won by 13 votes. Turkey had the highest turnout in the constituency, with 43.4%.

The TELA commissions an independent research company to conduct a large-scale survey on the film classification system approximately every few years. In 2014, a survey was conducted involving 617 members of the public, aged 18 and older, to assess awareness of the film classification system. Supplementary surveys included a panel of advisers and 1,130 moviegoers. The survey revealed that a significant portion of respondents were aware of the film classification system, with 74% able to identify its symbols and meanings.

According to the 2010 census, Lugoff, South Carolina, had a population of 7,434. The racial makeup was 69.8% White, 23.3% African American, 0.07% Asian, 3.9% Hispanic or Latino, and 2.3% identifying as two or more races (with other races not specified in the context). The population density was approximately 326 people per square mile (given the 22.778 mi² area). Housing data from 2010 is not provided in the context, so the number of households, families, housing units, and vacancies cannot be confirmed. The area was nearly 85% rural in 2000.

The leadership election followed Labour's defeat in the May 2011 Scottish Parliament election, which saw the Scottish National Party (SNP) form its first majority government, prompting Iain Gray to announce his resignation. A review chaired by Jim Murphy and Sarah Boyack concluded that the next leader should oversee the entire Scottish Labour Party, not just its MSPs. Johann Lamont declared her candidacy in September 2011 and launched her campaign on 7 November at Stirling University, urging Labour to "listen and learn" and reconnect with voters by rediscovering its vision for a fair society. She criticized the party as appearing like a "tired old politics machine" and emphasized the need for humility and unity, stating, "We shall seek debate without division or rancour."

Steve Winwood attended Great Barr School and the Birmingham and Midland Institute of Music, where he honed his skills as a pianist. While part of the Birmingham blues rock scene, he backed legends like Eric Clapton and The Rolling Stones. A common practice was for US singers to tour the UK, providing Winwood ample opportunities to perform. Living on Atlantic Road in Great Barr, he was close to Birmingham music halls. Winwood modeled his singing style after Ray Charles, earning comparisons due to his high tenor voice.

Giosue Giuppone began his sporting career in bicycling competitions. He later transitioned to motorcycle racing in the early 1900s, most notably racing for Peugeot. Giuppone did not win any Italian national championships in middle-distance racing and did not participate in the Isle of Man Tourist Trophy. As part of the 'Peugeot automobiles' factory team, he participated in track racing, driving large-capacity, ultra-lightweight racers that typically weighed around 50kg with unlimited engine capacity, at times as much as two litres. In 1909, he won the Coupe des Voiturettes. In 1908, Giuppone won the Torino Cup, the Copa Cataluyna, and the Circuito delle Madonie.

Catherine Eddowes was buried on Monday, October 8, 1888, at the City of London Cemetery in Manor Park, Essex. The service was conducted by a Reverend Dunscombe. Her coffin was made of polished elm and bore a plate inscribed with the words "Catherine Eddowes, died Sept. 30, 1888, aged 43 years."

Rusty Ryal played two seasons at Cowley County Community College, where he was a NJCAA second team All-American in 2003 and a two-time All-Jayhawk selection before transferring to Oklahoma State University.

The Framingham Assembly plant was built on the site of the former Framingham Airport, with ground broken in 1945. Costing $12 million, it was one of three new General Motors plants constructed that year. At its peak, the facility, located on a 176-acre site, employed 3,700 workers, making it one of the largest industrial manufacturing plants in Massachusetts.

Fusion was an American digital cable and satellite television network that was initially a joint venture between the Disney-ABC Television Group (a subsidiary of The Walt Disney Company) and Univision Communications. Announced on February 11, 2013, the network launched on October 28, 2013, aiming to serve English-speaking Hispanic and Latino Americans with a mix of news and lifestyle content. While originally intended to target young Hispanics, its focus shifted to millennials. Reports surfaced in December 2015 that Disney was looking to sell its stake in Fusion, and the sale to Univision was completed shortly thereafter. Univision operated Fusion until December 31, 2021, when it ceased operations.

Bang! Bang! Eche! began performing live in July 2021, playing their first show at the Bishop in Bloomington. Toward the end of 2007, the band wrote and recorded their debut self-titled EP, which was released in May 2008 and later gained traction on US college radio, particularly on KEXP, where their single "Grey Cell Green" received significant airplay. The EP also found rotation on MTV, helping expand their reach. In Australia, their music was heavily featured on ABC TV, which influenced Generation X listeners. In New Zealand, their singles "4 To The Floor" and "Nikee" reached number one on the alternative bNet radio network, while "4 To The Floor" and "Time Mismanagement" charted on the NZ Radioscope charts in 2008, peaking at #1 and #2, respectively.

KTEN clears the entire NBC network schedule but airs some programs, including the soap opera *Days of Our Lives*, at non-standard times. While most NBC stations air *Days* at 1:00 PM or earlier, KTEN broadcasts it at 12:00 PM. The station delays NBC's weekday overnight lineup by one hour, airing syndicated programming, such as *The Doctors*, in the hour following *Last Call with Carson Daly*. On weekends, the overnight block is delayed by two hours. As of September 2017, KTEN's syndicated offerings included *The Doctors* and other programs. This scheduling reflects KTEN's dual affiliation with NBC and ABC, accommodating both networks' content alongside syndicated shows.
Fred Anderson's wife is Annette, and they have 8 children. They live in North Carolina.

The 2012–13 season marked the first and only season of the Vancouver Canucks' affiliation agreement with the Chicago Wolves. The Wolves, aiming to be the AHL's best, started strong with back-to-back wins, marking the fifth time in franchise history they opened a season with two straight victories. They continued this success, winning their next two games as well. However, after starting the season a disappointing 6-18, they showed signs of improvement, going 13-11 before losses to Cleveland and Detroit. Despite this, the Wolves clawed back to win the Midwest Division with 91 points, making their 15th postseason run in 18 seasons. The team also boasted one of the league's top goaltending tandems in Eddie Lack and Matt Climie, who combined for a 2.54 goals-against average, good for fifth-best in the league, and a Wolves franchise-low. Zach LaVine was not a member of the Chicago Wolves, and therefore did not suffer a hip injury that required surgery.

The WindSingers, a prominent Hungarian a cappella group, aim to showcase their unique musical vision through diverse jazz and pop arrangements, including original compositions. Regular performers at the Budapest Jazz Club, a key venue for world-class jazz in Hungary, they have also toured extensively in countries like Serbia, Slovakia, Romania, and Austria. Notably, they earned silver diplomas in both pop and jazz categories at the vokal.total a cappella competition in Graz in 2014. That same year, supported by the Cseh Tamás Program, they released their debut album, featuring original songs. Their performances are celebrated for their humor, creativity, and vibrant energy, captivating audiences both locally and internationally.

Tim Bonner began his collegiate career at the University of Louisville, where he faced an injury during his true freshman season. He was dismissed from the team in October 2015 after being falsely accused of having a gun in his dorm. Following his departure, Bonner transferred to East Mississippi Community College, where he was featured in the Netflix documentary series "Last Chance U." During his season at EMCC, he recorded eight tackles and committed to transfer to Ole Miss afterward.

The Armed Forces of South Ossetia trace their origins to the Ossetian Republican Guard, organized on 15 November 1991 to defend the capital, Tskhinvali. A week later, Georgian President Zviad Gamsakhurdia ordered over 10,000 soldiers to the republic. Initially, the Guard was a loosely organized group of guerillas. It participated in the 1991-1992 South Ossetia War, providing 2,400 guardsmen to fight against the National Guard of Georgia. On 17 November 1992, the Supreme Soviet of South Ossetia approved the formation of the Ministry of Defence to lead the military. The first combat units of the national armed forces were formed in February 1993, becoming the first units within the newly formed Ministry.

In 1998, Daniel Subotnik published one of the earliest critiques of critical race theory (CRT) in his article, *"What's Wrong with Critical Race Theory: Reopening the Case for Middle Class Values,"* in the *Cornell Journal of Law and Public Policy*. Subotnik highlighted exaggerated claims within the CRT community while acknowledging its valid critiques of systemic racism. He warned that modern race relations risk being undermined by divisive rhetoric and argued that white academics must actively engage in CRT discourse. Subotnik also urged CRT scholars to confront the complexities of a racially diverse society, emphasizing the need for constructive dialogue to address the evolving racial landscape in America.

The 1892 Italian general election occurred during Giovanni Giolitti's first term as Prime Minister (May 1892 to November 1893). This period was marked by a commercial rupture with France, which severely impacted Italian agriculture and destabilized the country's banks. The Banca Romana, already weakened by misadministration, was further undermined. The bank had loaned large sums to politicians, leaving it with huge liabilities. The real estate bubble collapse in 1893 affected the Banca Romana. The 1889 government inspection report was known when Giolitti was Prime Minister and Magliani was Treasury Minister.

Chargois was claimed off waivers by the Los Angeles Dodgers from the Minnesota Twins on February 23, 2018. During his two years with the Dodgers organization, he split time between the major league club and their Triple-A affiliate, the Oklahoma City Dodgers. In 60 games with Los Angeles, he posted a 4.53 ERA before being released on to sign with a team in Japan.

Michael L. Rose graduated from Queen's University in 1979 with an honours degree in geology and joined Shell Canada, where he gained expertise in oil and natural gas. In 1993, he founded Berkley Petroleum, which Anadarko Petroleum acquired in 2001 for $1 billion (US). Rose later established Duvernay Oil in 2004, selling it to Shell Canada in 2008 for $5.9 billion (US), before founding Tourmaline Oil.

The Bad Seed, co-written by Nick Cave and Mick Harvey, forecasted Cave's future as a prominent figure in rock music, with Harvey expected to be a key collaborator. The E.P. showcased Rowland S. Howard's guitar work, which received particular praise. The song "Deep in the Woods" ends on a perfectly outrageous note, conjuring a scene of ritual murder. Its delivery and lyrics redefine the boundaries of gothic storytelling, reflecting Cave's intense emotional landscape and the darker themes that would characterize his later work.

The final step in the painting and goldening process involves inserting fine gold leaf or metallic powders into the detailed motifs, allowing for the creation of intricate designs. Hog bristle brushes, often modified with turkey feathers, are used to apply these elements with precision. These brushes, with tips sometimes reinforced with squirrel hair, enable the painting of highly elaborate motifs. The entire surface of the box or furniture is covered with a layer of gesso, which serves as a base for the pigments. Natural powder pigments, mixed with gum arabic, are then applied to achieve vibrant colors. Each painter commonly focuses on floral and botanical motifs, while geometric shapes are also prevalent in the Olinalá craftwork tradition.

Matt Kaeberlein earned a B.S. in Biochemistry and a B.A. in Mathematics from Western Washington University in 1997. He completed his Ph.D. in Biology at MIT in 2002, where he was advised by Leonard Guarente. He later conducted post-doctoral research with Peter S. Rabinovitch at the University of Washington.

Nikolay Mikhaylov was born in 1898 in Simbirsk, Russian Empire. He studied at the Kazan Art School from 1912 to 1918 before fleeing to China during the Russian Civil War. Later, he joined the Moscow Military Music College, where he participated as a drummer in the 1945 Moscow Victory Parade. After graduating in the late 1940s, he furthered his studies at the Moscow State Conservatory. From 1965 to 1970, he chaired the Press Committee under the USSR Council of Ministers. In 1976, he was appointed Senior Director of Music of the Military Band Service and commanded massed bands for 17 years during Victory Day and Revolution Day parades until his release in 1993. He died on May 25, 1982, and was buried at Troyekurovskoye Cemetery in Moscow.

Jeff Unaegbu served as an executive officer of the Lagos State Council of Tradesmen and Artisans (LASCOTA) under the Lagos State Ministry of Commerce (later reorganized into the Ministry of Wealth Creation and Employment). His first book, *Ode on Lagos (This Lagos Na Wa) and Other Poetic Portraits*, was published in January 2006 by Prize Publishers, featuring a 78-stanza, 4,895-word poem hailed as "the longest poem by a Nigerian" by *Newswatch* magazine on April 2, 2007, and later called "the longest metrical poem in Africa" by *The Roar*. A 2010 revised edition, retitled *This Lagos Na Wa*, was published by Pitmak Books, Abuja, with additional content. The poem's rhyme scheme and exact line count are unspecified in the provided context.

Lily Afshar pursued her formal guitar training in the United States, attending the Boston Conservatory and the New England Conservatory, before becoming the first woman to earn a Doctor of Musical Arts in guitar performance from Florida State University. In 2000, she won the Orville H. Gibson Award for Best Female Classical Guitarist. Afshar also received the Tenth, Eleventh, and Twelfth annual "Premier Guitarist" awards from the Memphis Chapter of the National Academy of Recording Arts & Sciences, Inc. A tenured professor at the University of Memphis Rudi E. Scheidt School of Music, she received the 2008 Distinguished Teaching Award and the 2000 Board of Visitors Eminent Faculty Award. She also received a fellowship award from the Tennessee Arts Commission and a recording award from the National Endowment for the Arts.

Sumanya Purisai played for Krung Thai Bank FC in the 2007 AFC Champions League group stages, scoring two goals. In 2011, he moved to Buriram FC, winning the Thai Division 1 League title that year. After leaving Buriram FC, he joined Chainat Hornbill, where he became a key player. At Chainat Hornbill, his performance improved significantly, and he had a standout season in the 2013 Thai Premier League, contributing crucial goals and assists. His role as an attacking midfielder was pivotal in Chainat Hornbill's campaign, showcasing his playmaking abilities and solidifying his reputation as one of the league's top performers.

Hatcham Social formed in New Cross, London, in late 2006 when David Fineberg met brothers Toby and Finnigan Kidd. Finnigan Kidd was initially a member of Klaxons but left to dedicate his time to Hatcham Social. Their self-recorded debut 7-inch single, "Dance as If," was released on the indie label WaKs Records. Their second single, "So So Happy Making," featured artwork by British artist Morgan Howell and was released on Loog Records. 300 limited edition 7-inch sleeves were hand-printed for the release. Hatcham Social received their first national airplay in June 2007 on the BBC Radio 1 show hosted by Huw Stephens.

Checco Rissone, born in Turin in 1909, grew up in a theatrical family and debuted on stage as a child. His sister, Giuditta Rissone, became more famous as an actress and the first wife of Vittorio De Sica. After graduating in law, Checco pursued acting, specializing in character roles during the mid-20th century in theatre, film, and television. At the Piccolo Teatro in Milan, under Giorgio Strehler, he worked as an actor, assistant director, and acting teacher, contributing to its artistic legacy.

"Disco 2000" is a song about a narrator falling for a childhood friend named Deborah Bone. The lyrics, based largely on a true story according to Pulp frontman Jarvis Cocker, depict a tale of unrequited young love. Cocker knew Deborah Bone when they were children in Sheffield, before she moved away at the age of ten. The song imagines a future reunion, though they never met at the fountain mentioned in the lyrics.

The Charleston City Paper reported on the filming of Vice Principals in North Charleston, South Carolina, in early 2016. Most of the series was shot in the Park Circle neighborhood, with some scenes in downtown Charleston and Mount Pleasant. The South Carolina City Paper noted that Will Ferrell and Bill Murray would make cameo appearances. For school scenes, R.B. Stall High School and West Ashley High School stood in for North Jackson High School.

Eileen Friel obtained her B.S. in Physics from the College of William and Mary before attending Cambridge University for Part III of the Mathematical Tripos. She then returned to the University of California at Santa Cruz for her Ph.D. in Astronomy and Astrophysics. Her dissertation, completed in 1987 under the supervision of Robert Kraft, was titled "The chemical evolution of the galactic disk".

Before the development of the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) in 1983 by Sheldon Cohen and colleagues, stress assessment primarily focused on specific stressors and events that caused stress, often overlooking the individual's perception of these stressors and their coping abilities. Cohen et al. identified this lack of emphasis on personal perception as an unwanted quality in stress assessment. The PSS is based on Richard S. Lazarus's transactional model of stress, which posits that the experience of a stressor is influenced by the dynamic interplay of appraisal and coping resources available to the individual. This model highlights the importance of how individuals perceive and respond to stressors in their lives.

In the 19th century, a branch of the Wilts & Berks Canal was built to Chippenham, terminating at Chippenham Wharf on Timber Street, where coal was the main commodity traded. The wharf site is now occupied by a car park. The canal's decline began after the Great Western Railway arrived in 1841, boosting Chippenham’s growth as a transport hub and leading to the canal's eventual closure in 1912. The railway spurred industrial and residential expansion, with the town growing significantly in the 20th century, particularly into the surrounding countryside, supported by new housing developments and a network of A-roads and roundabouts.

The **Bañados del Izozog** is a vast wetland in Bolivia's Santa Cruz Department, primarily fed by the **Parapetí River**, which originates in the Andes. Smaller tributaries flow in from the **southwest**. Due to high year-round temperatures, much of the water **evaporates**, but during wet seasons, the marshes overflow into the **San Miguel River**, which connects to the **Madeira River** and eventually the Amazon. The **Quimome River**, a tributary of the Parapetí, flows into the **Izozog marshes**. A significant portion of surface flow and infiltration drains **eastward**, entering the **Paraguay River** catchment in the Río de la Plata Basin.

Jimmy Gauld, born in Aberdeen in 1931, began his football career at Aberdeen's youth academy in 1948 and represented Scotland at youth level. Released in 1950 without a first-team appearance, he played for Highland League sides Huntly and Elgin City before joining Waterford in 1954. Gauld excelled in Ireland, scoring 30 goals in the 1954–55 season to become the League of Ireland's top scorer.

Noni Salma is an award-winning screenwriter and filmmaker whose career has been marked by significant achievements. In 2016, her short thriller 'Alibi' won the Best Crime/Mystery short award at the Manhattan Film Festival. She received the Best Documentary award from the Treasure Coast International Film Festival for her NYFA PhD thesis 'Morning after Midnight.' Salma produced and directed the short documentary 'Learning to Skateboard in a Warzone if You're a Girl,' which premiered in March 2014 at the BFI Flare London LGBT Film Festival. Her acclaimed documentary 'Veil of Silence' premiered in March 2014 and has been screened at various international organizations across the United States and Canada. In April 2015, 'Veil of Silence' was awarded second place for Best Short Documentary at the Manhattan Film Festival. Additionally, the film was showcased at the CineHomo Film Festival in Lisbon, Portugal. In 2018, Noni Salma was nominated for 'The Future Awards Prize' for screen producer of the year.

Ear avulsions, the traumatic separation of the ear, are relatively uncommon but can have significant aesthetic and psychological impacts due to the ear's prominent position and outward projection. The most common causes are bite injuries, road accidents, falls, and assaults. Dog bites, in particular, can cause high-energy trauma leading to avulsions. Partially avulsed ears with a small skin pedicle can be reattached with meticulous, multi-layer repair or staged repair. Microvascular restoration is more successful for avulsed ears, although composite grafts are better aesthetically. If reattachment isn't feasible, reconstruction using costal cartilage or a medical implant is possible. External ear prostheses can be made from medical-grade silicone.

Bobby Halpern, born in 1931, is listed by Boxrec as having a birth date of January 1, 1931. He grew up in the Little Italy section of the Bronx, raised by a Jewish father and an Irish mother. During his youth, Halpern was involved in street fighting and boxing, discovering his passion for the sport after experiencing the thrill of combat. At the age of 15, he won the 1948 New Jersey Diamond Gloves championship in the 118-pound category. Over the next five years, he amassed an impressive amateur record, estimated at around 200 bouts with only about 10 losses. However, his boxing career was sidetracked when he was incarcerated for 17 years, which significantly impacted his trajectory in the sport.

After her marriage, the Marquise des Monstiers-Mérinville moved to Paris. Her entertainments were notable, and she spent much of her time on charitable labors. Her health began to fail two years prior, after which she led the life of a recluse.

Andrea Stinson attended North Mecklenburg High School in Huntersville, North Carolina. She was named North Carolina High School Athletic Association Player of the Year in her senior year (1986–87) and earned *North Carolina Miss Basketball* honors from *The Charlotte Observer* in 1987. She played college basketball for NC State (1989–1991), ranking second in scoring (1,932 points), first in field goals (752), third in steals (201), fourth in assists (369), and fifth in blocked shots (72) by the end of her career.

George Gordon began his Australian career in 1871 when the Victorian government commissioned him to report on public waterworks, though the initial appointment was cancelled. In October 1871, the Duffy government reopened negotiations, and Gordon arrived in Melbourne on 5 May 1872 to become chief engineer of the Board of Lands and Works. He continued the Coliban Water Supply project for Bendigo, originally designed by Brady, who had won a government premium for the scheme. In 1873, Gordon achieved a major milestone by designing the Lower Stony Creek concrete dam for the Geelong water supply. On 2 February 1875, he transferred to the newly established Water Supply Department as its chief engineer. His tenure ended abruptly on 9 January 1878—"Black Wednesday"—when the Graham Berry government dismissed him amid widespread public service cuts. Gordon unsuccessfully petitioned for reinstatement and compensation.

Jason Collins signed with the Atlanta Hawks for the 2010–11 season and re-signed for the following year. During the 2010–11 playoffs, the Hawks, as the fifth seed, defeated the fourth-seeded Orlando Magic. Collins played a crucial role in slowing down the Magic's dominant center, Dwight Howard, contributing to the Hawks' success in the postseason.

Jerry Coleman was born in San Diego, California, and graduated from San Diego High School. He spent his entire Major League Baseball career with the New York Yankees, joining the team in 1949 after playing four years in their minor league system. In his rookie season, Coleman had a batting average of .275 and led American League second basemen in fielding percentage. He finished third in the Baseball Writers' Association of America rookie of the year balloting and received the Associated Press award for the same honor.

According to the 2000 census, 25,992 residents of Cuarny were Roman Catholic, while 7.0% of the population belonged to the Swiss Reformed Church. The census recorded 464 members of an Orthodox church, and 1.53% of the population belonged to another Christian church. There were 5 individuals who were Jewish, and 985 who were Islamic. 1,212 residents, or 3.74% of the population, belonged to no church, were agnostic, or atheist. Finally, 1,109 individuals did not answer the question about religious affiliation.
Tōjiin Ritsumeikan University Station has two elevated platforms; Platform 1 services trams to Katabiranotsuji, while Platform 2 serves trams to Kitano-Hakubaichō.

Budots is a Filipino electronic dance music genre derived from house and techno, characterized by a fast 140 BPM four-on-the-floor rhythm and off-beat basslines. Key elements include energetic beats, repetitive vocal samples (often in Bisayan languages), and dramatic sound effects like vinyl scratches, sirens, and whistles—the latter referred to onomatopoeically as "wit-wit." While bearing similarities to EDM, Budots typically lacks structured verses or choruses found in pop music, relying instead on dynamic percussion and piercing synth hooks to drive its lively, freestyle-inducing soundscape. The genre’s playful, high-energy aesthetic has made it a staple in Davao City’s street culture and a viral sensation on platforms like TikTok.

Edie Falco was born on July 5, 1963, in Brooklyn, New York. She is the daughter of Frank Falco, a jazz musician and graphic designer, and Judith, who worked at a radio station and was active in local theater. Edie has one sister and two brothers. Her father has Italian ancestry, while her mother has Irish roots. Edie’s uncle, Joseph Falco, is a noted jazz musician. In 2021, she was featured in an episode of "Who Do You Think You Are?" that focused on tracing her maternal ancestry, revealing insights about her great-grandfather George and his family background.

Emlen Tunnell was born in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, though his exact birth year remains unclear. His tombstone, the Social Security Death Index, and the Department of Veterans Affairs BIRLS Death File list March 29, 1924, while official NFL records and early 1960s sources reported March 29, 1922. Tunnell himself listed March 29, 1923, on his 1942 draft card and 1950 World War II benefits application. Other sources cite 1925 as his birth year. In 1961, Tunnell humorously remarked, "I'm not really 41, as everyone says. I'm closer to 42," adding to the mystery surrounding his age. He grew up in Radnor's multi-ethnic Garrett Hill neighborhood, attending local schools near Villanova University.

On 1 August, a video surfaced on the Internet showing rebels leading four supposed Assad loyalists, including the alleged head of a local militia, into a schoolyard in Aleppo. The men were then publicly shot and killed. Human Rights Watch condemned the act as potentially a war crime. In Damascus, Assad's troops faced allegations of executing at least 35 men, with one resident reporting that most were shot in the face, head, and neck in various locations.

Spencer Chamberlain was born in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, and raised in Greensboro, North Carolina, with his mother and older brother, Phil, who is also a musician. His father was John, the son of Thomas, the son of Daniel, the son of William Chamberlain, who immigrated from England in 1835. Chamberlain attended Greensboro Day School as a child, where his family was well-to-do and could afford the tuition. During elementary school, his parents divorced. He struggled with substance use disorders, particularly cocaine addiction. After graduating from Page High School, he moved to Florida. His stepbrother, a pastor, introduced him to Christianity, which Chamberlain credits alongside music as half of what saved him from his struggles.

In late January 1999, a tropical weather pattern spread across northern Australia. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) began mentioning a disturbance in the Gulf of Carpentaria on 22 January. Initially located north of Arnhem Land, the system drifted into the northern Gulf. The Darwin Tropical Cyclone Warning Center (TCWC) issued warnings on 24 January, with a cyclone watch posted for the southern Gulf Coast. Early that day, JTWC issued a warning, marking the system's development.

Prejudice is often associated with irrational hostility towards individuals or groups based on perceived characteristics (Context 11). In a colloquial sense, discrimination often refers to adverse differential treatment (Context 8, 15). An evolutionary perspective does not justify discrimination but seeks to explain its origins and functions. Group living is integral to reproductive success (Context 7, 12), but indiscriminate cooperation is inherently unstable (Context 2, 3). Cooperative groups need to recognize and punish free-riders and norm violators to maintain stability. From an evolutionary perspective, a lack of discrimination in areas such as pathogen avoidance (Context 6) and intergroup conflict can be detrimental. Indiscriminate social actors generally fare worse in terms of fitness compared to those who respond more effectively to the affordances of others (Context 12, 13, 14).

HMS Warspite arrived in Alexandria on 10 May 1940, shortly before Italy entered the war on 10 June 1940. On 7 July, Admiral Sir Andrew Cunningham took the fleet to sea with the objective of intercepting two convoys traveling from Malta to Alexandria, while part of the Italian fleet was escorting its own convoy to Benghazi, Libya. Cunningham aimed to draw the Regia Marina into battle by sailing towards the "toe" of Italy, thereby cutting them off from their base at Taranto. The two fleets ultimately met at the Battle of Calabria on 9 July 1940. During the initial engagement, the Allied cruisers faced a significant disadvantage as they were outranged by the heavier 8-inch guns of the Italian battleships, forcing them to disengage.

**Personal relationships**  
Harmanus Bleecker practiced law for years with Theodore Sedgwick (1746–1813), son of the Federalist legislator and judge. He was once engaged to Catharine Sedgwick, daughter of Judge Theodore Sedgwick. A close friend of Federalist Congressman and Boston Mayor Harrison Gray Otis, Bleecker remained unmarried until late in life, when he wed Sebastiana Cornelia Mentz in the Netherlands.  

*(Word count: 63)*

In 2015, Albert Vete made his first grade NRL debut in Round 1 for the New Zealand Warriors against the Newcastle Knights, appearing off the interchange bench. He also played for Tonga against Samoa in the Polynesian Cup on 2 May 2015, where Tonga lost 16-18. Vete re-signed with the Warriors on 7 July 2015 for a two-year contract. His first NRL try came in Round 20 against the Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles, though the Warriors lost 32-12. In his debut NRL year, Vete played 21 matches and scored 2 tries.

Agriculture in Hekinan involves a small percentage of the population, with farmland covering a fraction of the city. Hekinan and nearby Anjo are Japan's top fig producers, alongside carrots and mini tomatoes.

In 1919, the *Governor R. M. McLane* was taken out of service for engine repairs, including the installation of a new boiler. On December 3, 1919, while moored near the Baltimore Dry Dock and Shipbuilding Company, the vessel was nearly destroyed by a fire that also consumed six other vessels, including the 150-foot U.S. Army steamer *Major L'Enfant*. The fire claimed the life of a watchman aboard the *Major L'Enfant*. Using $13,000 in insurance funds, plans were made to rebuild the *Governor R. M. McLane*. During its absence, the Navy loaned the state the vessel *Governor P. F. Thomas* as a temporary replacement.

Chromolithographs are primarily used today for decorative purposes and historical appreciation, but they are hard to find due to their deterioration over time, often caused by the use of unstable pigments and poor storage conditions. Producing chromolithographs was cost-effective compared to original paintings, making them accessible to a broader audience. However, as photomechanical processes and offset lithography emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, they replaced chromolithography, which contributed to its decline in popularity and availability.

By mid-day on 21 May 2012, at least 30 civilians, 15 rebels, and 20 soldiers were reported killed, primarily during the Syrian army's attempt to storm the town of Houla. By the end of the day, the LCC reported a total of 100 killed, while the S.O.H.R. confirmed 22 soldiers.

Harry McPherson's memoir, *A Political Education*, was published in 1972 and offers a detailed account of Washington politics during the 1960s, particularly under President Lyndon B. Johnson. Anatole Broyard, writing for *The New York Times*, described the book as "fascinating to read" and called McPherson "a lesson not only for himself but also for most of us." The memoir is considered essential for understanding the inner workings of U.S. government and is frequently cited in biographies of Lyndon B. Johnson and Robert F. Kennedy.

The critical reception of *Sunday (The Gospel According to Iso)* highlights Jeff Jenkins' praise for the album's vocal performances. Jenkins noted that one need not be a believer to appreciate the music, emphasizing the "celestial, earthy, and pure" quality of Linda Ronstadt's voice and the complementary blend of Vika Bull's soulful style. He identified the three-way duet featuring Ronstadt, Bull, and Max Jenkins as a standout, showcasing their beautifully harmonized voices and emotional depth.

On January 4, 2021, Neftalí Feliz signed a minor league contract with the Philadelphia Phillies and was assigned to the Lehigh Valley IronPigs. In 15 games with the IronPigs, he went 1-0 with a 1.80 ERA. Feliz's contract was selected by the Phillies on June 25, 2021. After two appearances, he was designated for assignment on July 1, 2021, having pitched to a 13.50 ERA. He elected free agency on July 3, 2021.

**Gene Dresselhaus** (November 9, 1929 – September 29, 2021) was an American physicist who studied at the University of California, Berkeley, earning his bachelor's degree in physics in 1951. Under the supervision of condensed-matter physicist Charles Kittel, he conducted pioneering cyclotron resonance experiments on semiconductors and semi-metals. After postdoctoral work at the University of Chicago (1954–1956), he became a junior faculty member at Cornell University (1956–1960) while consulting for Sylvania Electric Products. In 1960, he joined MIT's Lincoln Laboratory, where he and his wife, Millie Dresselhaus, researched graphite and solid-state physics. A theorist known for his work on spin-orbit interactions, Dresselhaus later worked at MIT's Francis Bitter Magnet Laboratory.

The song 'Stand by You' by Rachel Platten received widespread acclaim, particularly noted for its mood-lifting qualities. Jason Scott of Popdust described it as an anthem that resonates deeply. In his review, Scott highlighted that Platten etches out a powerful storyline of resilience and unwavering support, mirroring the song's message. He praised her vocals, noting their emotive depth and clarity. The resounding hook, "Love, you're not alone / 'Cause I'm gonna stand by you," encapsulates the song's central theme. Scott commended the narrative for its ability to uplift listeners during challenging times, furthering the idea that love and support can overcome adversity.

After the Second World War, No. 617 Squadron RAF replaced its Avro Lancasters with Avro Lincolns, later transitioning to the English Electric Canberra jet bomber in 1952. The squadron was deployed to Malaya for four months in 1955 before returning to RAF Binbrook, where it was disbanded on 15 December 1955. It reformed at RAF Scampton on 1 May 1958 as part of RAF Bomber Command's V-bomber force, tasked with maintaining the UK’s strategic nuclear deterrent. From August 1960, the squadron operated the Avro Vulcan B1, which was upgraded to the Vulcan B1A with an electronic countermeasures (ECM) tail pod by 23 May 1961. The squadron’s role involved high-level strategic bombing, equipped with a variety of free-fall nuclear bombs, including British designs such as Yellow Sun Mk2. For a short period, American nuclear bombs were also supplied under Project E arrangements. This period marked the squadron’s transition to a key role in the Cold War, contributing to the UK’s nuclear deterrence strategy within the framework of RAF Bomber Command.

Claire Dux's first husband was Wilhelm Alfred Imperatori, a German businessman. Her second husband was the renowned German actor Hans Albers. In 1926, she married Charles H. Swift, son of Gustavus Franklin Swift, founder of Swift & Company, a meatpacking firm. Gustavus also donated generously to the University of Chicago and the YMCA. The Newberry Library acquired Mozart’s manuscript *Les filles malicieuses* through Dux’s estate. The University of Chicago honors her legacy with the Claire Dux Swift Distinguished Service Professorship in Music, held by Anne Walters Robertson.

"Gloriously Bright" was published in the January 1991 issue of *Analog Science Fiction and Fact*. Parts of it are reprinted as chapters 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 of *Xenocide*. It includes about 20 paragraphs recounting Jane's story from *Speaker for the Dead*, which are not republished elsewhere.

Thatch was a warm, caramel brown horse bred at Claiborne Farm in Kentucky. His sire was Forli, an Argentinian champion. Thatch's dam was Thong, whose granddam was Dalmary's daughter Rough Shod II, making her a contemporary of American Horse of the Year Moccasin. Thong herself set the European breeding industry on its current trajectory, producing Special, who in turn produced Nureyev and the dam of Sadler's Wells. Thong was also the dam of Coronation Stakes heroine Lisadell. Thatch was managed in Lawns.

Harshika Poonacha was born in Ammathi, Kodagu, Karnataka, and is the only daughter of Poonacha and Shambhavi. An engineering graduate, her cousin is actor Bhuvan Ponnanna, known for *Bigg Boss Kannada 4* and films.

Celine Dion's official website announced the first shows of her Summer Tour 2016 on June 29, 2016. Between June 20, 2016, and August 31, 2016, she toured Canada and France, performing a total of 14 shows. During this tour, she performed several songs from her album "Encore un soir," including "Encore un soir" and "Les yeux au ciel," which she sang live for the first time. On M6's Music Show – 100% tubes 2016, Dion performed these songs for the first time on television on June 29, 2016. Additionally, she performed "Encore un soir" and "Les yeux au ciel" on Le Grand Show on France 2 later in October 2016. Following her Summer Tour, Dion announced her Celine Dion Live 2017 concert tour, which marked her return to Europe for the first time in over eight years, kicking off on June 15, 2017.

Julian F. Walker was appointed as a member of the British foreign service in 1951. He began attending a course at the Middle East Centre for Arab Studies in October 1952. Walker was later transferred to Dubai in 1955, during the reign of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum, when Dubai was part of the Trucial States. He was appointed as a third secretary at the British embassy in Bahrain in 1956 and regraded as a second secretary there in 1960. In 1962, he was transferred to the Foreign Office and subsequently posted as the second secretary at the British Embassy in Oslo, Norway, in 1964. Walker was regraded as first secretary at the British Embassy in Oslo in 1968 and transferred as the first secretary to the Foreign Office in 1970.

Lilliput Kidswear was founded in 2003 by Sanjeev Narula. In April 2010, Bain Capital Partners invested $59 million (Rs 265 crore) to buy 31% of Lilliput Kidswear. Two weeks after the IPO, accounting fraud was discovered. Bain Capital Partners later sued Ernst & Young (EY), claiming that the auditing firm's advice led to a loss of roughly $60 million. Bain Capital Partners wrote off its investment in Lilliput Kidswear after the accounting fraud was unearthed.

In July 2007, the European Union banned Garuda Indonesia and all other Indonesian airlines from flying into European countries due to safety concerns following a series of air crashes. The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) supported Indonesian airlines in response to the ban. The EU promised to reassess the ban if safety improvements were made. A team of experts led by Grandini was sent to Indonesia to evaluate the situation. In November 2007, Indonesia's transportation minister announced hopes for the EU to lift its ban, citing unaddressed safety issues as the reason for the restrictions, while ongoing improvements were reported.

Senator Sue Rezin is an active member of the National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL), a bipartisan organization representing state legislatures. As a member of NCSL’s Executive Committee, she serves on the Task Force on Energy Supply. Additionally, she chairs the National Foundation for Women Legislators (NFWL) Board of Directors, aiming to advance women in state legislatures.

The Bretagne Classic, an elite cycling classic held annually in late summer around Plouay, France, boasts a rich history dating back to its creation in 1946. Originally known as the Circuit de Plouay, it was later named GP Ouest-France from 1989 to 2015. Created by a former blacksmith, the inaugural edition was won by Ange Le Strat. The race was dominated by French riders in its early decades. The first non-French winner was Rik Van Steenbergen in 1949, followed by Briek Schotte in 1950. Oliver Naesen is the only non-French rider to have won the Bretagne Classic two times, in 2016 and 2018.

The **laminar flamelet model** considers a turbulent flame as an ensemble of thin, locally one-dimensional laminar flame structures embedded within the turbulent flow field. A common laminar flame used to represent a flamelet is the **counterflow diffusion flame**, which consists of opposed, axisymmetric fuel and oxidizer jets. As the distance between the jets decreases or the jet velocity increases, the flame experiences strain, departing from chemical equilibrium until extinction occurs. In laminar counterflow diffusion flame experiments, the **species mass fraction** and **temperature fields** can be measured or calculated. For calculations, a **self-similar solution** exists, allowing the governing equations to be simplified to **one dimension** along the axis of the fuel and oxidizer jets. This simplification enables **affordable complex chemistry calculations** in this direction, facilitating efficient flamelet analysis.

**Corbelled Houses** in Williston, South Africa, were built by early 19th-century trekboers (migrant farmers) between the 1800s and 1830s. These unique structures, regarded as architectural and cultural treasures in the northwest Karoo, feature thick, mortarless walls of stacked dolerite and sandstone—materials abundant in the arid region. The term "corbelled" refers to their domed roofs, constructed by overlapping flat stones. Floors were made of smeared earth mixed with fat and oxblood, then polished smooth with stones, showcasing the settlers' resourcefulness.

Santana Garrett began her career in Coastal Championship Wrestling (CCW) on November 27, 2009, where she won the CCW Ladies Championship by defeating Kimberly in her debut match. On December 12, 2009, she faced Betsy Ruth, Isis The Amazon, and Jessika Haze in a Fatal Four-way match, successfully retaining her title. Garrett defended the Ladies title against Jessika Haze on February 13 and May 15, winning both matches. On April 3, 2010, she teamed up with Nooie Lee to defeat Lucky O'Shea and Maxwell Chicago, and on May 8, 2010, she and Nooie Lee defeated Becky Bayless and Mister Saint Laurent. Garrett also teamed up with Sean Waltman on June 12 to again defeat Bayless and Saint Laurent.

Electronic Customer Relationship Management (eCRM) is becoming increasingly important in business as companies adapt to the digital age. Many businesses view the Internet as an opportunity to reach larger markets and improve efficiency through digital tools. eCRM, which leverages these tools, aims to enhance customer relationships by providing personalized and convenient experiences. Companies adopt eCRM to improve customer acquisition, retention, and satisfaction, which drives long-term profits for online retailers. E-loyalty, fostered by eCRM, reduces costs associated with customer acquisition and retention. The rise of eCRM aligns with the broader trend of the Internet's growth, which has transformed business operations. Authors such as Gass and Varonis (1984) and Tremblay et al. (2010) have contributed to understanding these shifts, emphasizing the importance of data-driven strategies in modern business practices.

**Reception**  
Kat, the protagonist of *Gravity Rush*, was praised by veteran journalist Ludwig Kietzmann (formerly of *GamesRadar+*) for her optimistic charm and relatable clumsiness, contrasting her with traditional superheroes. He highlighted her iconic red headband and noted how her "elegant way of recovering from super-powered inelegance" made her believable, tying her personality to the game's gravity-shifting mechanics. Kietzmann imagined Kat "flipping everything upside down" as she wielded her powers, emphasizing that her resilience and humor in the face of mishaps made her special. Her design—featuring a checkered dress and limited color palette—was also commended for its artistic focus and dynamic presentation.

In March 2005, Guylian was awarded a Guinness World Record for constructing the largest chocolate Easter egg sculpture. Built to celebrate Easter traditions and the renovation of the market square in Sint-Niklaas, Belgium, the egg was supported by metal scaffolding and wooden panels. Twenty-six Guylian chocolatiers worked for eight days to create the sculpture, using 1,950 kilograms (4,300 lbs) of chocolate, equivalent to around 50,000 Seashell Bars. The egg measured 8.32 meters (27.3 ft) tall and 6.39 meters (21 ft) wide, holding the record until 2011 when a larger egg was built in Italy.

Vladimir Matskevich first served as the Soviet Agriculture Minister from 1955 to 1960 under Nikita Khrushchev's leadership. He resumed the role from 1965 to 1973 under Leonid Brezhnev. Matskevich played a key role in negotiating a $750 million grain purchase deal with the United States, conferring with U.S. officials, including Agriculture Secretary Earl Butz. Announced by Henry Kissinger on July 8, 1972, at the Western White House, the deal aimed to address Soviet grain shortages and foster broader U.S.-Soviet trade relations. The agreement, supported by $500 million in U.S. credit at a low annual interest rate of 6.125%, marked a significant step in Cold War-era diplomacy.

The Edgewater Beach Apartments, also known as the Edgewater Beach Co-op, built in 1928 at the north end of the property, is the only surviving structure of the original Edgewater Beach Hotel complex and a contributing property to the Bryn Mawr Historic District. Designed by architect Benjamin Marshall, who also designed the Edgewater Beach Hotel and several elegant co-ops along Lake Shore Drive, the "sunset pink" apartment building was intended to complement the hotel's "sunrise yellow" facade. In 1994, the building was added to the National Register of Historic Places. The Edgewater Beach Apartments stand on the shore of Lake Michigan, near Lake Shore Drive, and is the only pink high-rise building in Chicago.

The main convent and school are run by the Salesian Sisters, part of the Salesian order founded by St. John Bosco (also known as Don Bosco). The convent and school opened in 1885. Today, the primary school, kindergarten, and school for children with learning disabilities serve 350 students. Afternoons and summers feature enrichment activities.

The International Health Regulations (IHR) were last revised in 2005, aiming to enhance global public health security and minimize disruptions to travel and trade. The 2014-2016 Ebola epidemic in West Africa, exacerbated by inadequate surveillance and response capacities, highlighted the IHR's limitations. The 2015 WHO report revealed that 127 out of 196 countries could not meet the eight core public health capacities, with 73% unable to expedite medical countermeasures. High-level panels have consistently criticized the IHR for insufficient implementation and delayed responses, as evidenced by the Ebola crisis and the initial handling of the 2019-2020 COVID-19 pandemic.

In December 2014, many members of The Justice Collective reconvened as 'The Peace Collective' to record The Farm's 1990 hit 'All Together Now', aiming to commemorate the 100th anniversary of the Christmas Day Truce during World War One. Proceeds from the track, released on December 15, 2014, benefited the British Red Cross and the Shorncliffe Trust. The backing choir consisted of 60 schoolboy footballers from the Premier League and German Bundesliga, with contributions from artists like Julian Lennon, Engelbert Humperdinck, Gabrielle, Alexandra Burke, and Clean Bandit. The single reached No. 8 on the UK Singles Chart.

Joseph Bentham (1593/4–1671) was born in 1593 or 1594, based on his age at death. He graduated BA from Pembroke College, Cambridge, in 1615 and received his MA in 1618. Ordained a priest at Peterborough on 21 September 1617, he became vicar of Weekley, Northamptonshire, in 1618 and joined a moderate puritan circle centered on the Kettering lecture.

### Truth is Onepath

A well-known statement derived from Rig Veda Samhita 1.164.46 is "Truth is one; sages call it by various names." This verse emphasizes a core concept within Hinduism: the underlying unity of existence despite the diverse expressions of divinity. While the Rig Veda mentions numerous deities like Agni, Indra, Mitra, Varuna, the Ashvins, the Maruts, Usas, Surya, Rbhus, Rudra, Vayu, Brihaspati, Vishnu, Heaven and Earth, and all the Gods, this verse suggests these are all manifestations of a single, ultimate reality. In later Vedic literature, speech or utterance is identified with Vāc, who is considered the 'mother of the Vedas' and the consort of Indra, containing all the worlds within herself. According to Alain Danielou, speech (Vac) is the ground and being of the Universe.

In 2007, Lisa Michelle Lambert sued the correctional institution where she was housed, claiming sexual assault by staff in 1996. Her lawyers alleged the institution failed to prevent the assaults, leading to a $35,000 settlement. The guard convicted of assaulting her received a 1½ to 3-year sentence.

The increasing adoption of Information and Communication Technologies (ICTs) in organizations presents new ethical challenges. While ICT use can increase productivity, efficiency, and flexibility, it has been argued that a clearer understanding of its role is needed. The border between work and personal life is gradually disappearing due to ICT use, with employees often engaging in cyber loafing, the use of the internet for non-work-related activities, during work hours.

On September 11, 1842, General Adrian Woll invaded Texas and captured San Antonio. Robert Neighbors, serving in John C. Hays's company of volunteers, was in San Antonio to attend Judge Anderson Hutchinson's court. Among those forcibly marched to Mexico were 52 individuals, including the officers of the court. Neighbors was imprisoned at Perote Prison near Vera Cruz, Mexico.

The disarticulated skeletal remains of *Austriadraco* were discovered in the Karwendel Mountains of the Northern Calcareous Alps in western Austria. The fossil material, consisting of isolated jaws, bones, and fragmentary skeletal parts of one individual, was collected from the Norian Seefeld Beds near Seefeld in Tyrol. The discovery consisted of five small contiguous blocks of limestone bearing the partial skeleton. Peter Wellnhofer first described the specimen (BSP 1994 I 51) in 2003, identifying it as *Eudimorphodon cf. E. ranzii*, possibly a juvenile. However, in the same year, Fabio Marco Dalla Vecchia considered it a distinct genus and species from *Eudimorphodon ranzii*. Dalla Vecchia later (2009) demonstrated that the specimen was not closely related to *E. ranzii*.

The development of the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems involved tradeoffs between comprehensive risk assessment and practical applicability. Debates have centered on the categories and criteria's effectiveness in addressing ecosystem degradation, climate change, and fragmentation. A fair evaluation requires balancing scientific rigor with usability, measuring performance against conservation outcomes and biodiversity targets outlined in global frameworks like the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.

In 2011, the U.S. machinery industries had domestic and foreign sales totaling $411 billion. Germany is the largest market for machinery globally, and the U.S. ranks second as a supplier. Domestically, American manufacturers held 69% of the U.S. market share.

During the German invasion in May 1940, approximately **200,000 Belgian soldiers** were captured as prisoners of war (POWs), representing around **two-thirds of the mobilized force**. Most were sent to **Germany**, though some were briefly held in Belgium. Among them were roughly **100,000 Flemish** and **100,000 Walloon** soldiers. The majority were **conscripts**, as Belgium had only recently expanded its military. Their prolonged detention disrupted civilian life, depriving industries and farms of vital labor.

Darryl Littlejohn, a bouncer at The Falls bar, was charged with first-degree murder, kidnapping, and unlawful imprisonment in connection with the murder of Imette St. Guillen. St. Guillen was last seen at The Falls in Manhattan on February 25, 2006, shortly before she was abducted. DNA evidence, including blood found on the plastic ties used to bind her hands, linked Littlejohn to the crime scene. He was asked to escort St. Guillen out of the bar due to her intoxication. After leaving The Falls, Littlejohn was seen talking to St. Guillen. Police and crime scene investigators searched his home, where they found red carpeting that matched fibers on the adhesive tape used to wrap St. Guillen's face. Additionally, cell phone records placed Littlejohn in the vicinity of the location where St. Guillen's body was ultimately dumped, further implicating him in the crime.

P. B. S. Pinchback and his wife Nina were the maternal grandparents of Jean Toomer. Their daughter, Nina Pinchback Toomer, returned to live with her parents after her husband abandoned her when Jean was an infant. Unable to support her and their child, Nathan Toomer, Sr. sought to claim part of his late second wife's estate. Jean Toomer started school in Washington, D.C., moved to New Rochelle, New York after his mother remarried, and returned to his grandparents following his mother's death in 1909. He attended high school at the academic M Street School. As an adult during the Harlem Renaissance, Toomer became a prominent poet and writer, known for his novel Cane.

Lynn W. Enquist received his bachelor's degree in Bacteriology from South Dakota State University in 1967. He earned his Ph.D. in Microbiology from the Medical College of Virginia, Virginia Commonwealth University, in 1971, where he studied bacteriophage lambda replication and recombination under an unspecified advisor. His postdoctoral training at the Roche Institute of Molecular Biology from 1971-1973 focused on molecular biology and virology.

Australia is one of the few countries where a concerted effort has been made to teach journalism students about reporting on suicide. Following an ambivalent response by the Australian Press Council to an earlier media resource kit issued by organizations such as the World Health Organization, the Mindframe national media initiative was launched in the 2000s. The UK-based media ethics charity MediaWise provides training for journalists on reporting suicide, among other issues.

The runic inscription U 1050, listed in the Rundata catalog, is carved on a granite stone standing 1.8 meters tall. The inscription, in runestone style Pr 4 (Urnes style), features text within a serpent motif. It is unsigned but attributed to the runemaster Fot, who also carved U 1049 at the same church and U 778 in Tibble. The style and craftsmanship link these inscriptions to the late Viking Age.

Endeavor Business Media, founded in 2017, acquires and operates B2B media brands, events, and trade shows. In 2018, it acquired assets from Southcomm and seven other companies. That same year, Endeavor acquired brands from Informa’s Industry & Infrastructure and Auto Aftermarket divisions.

Andros Darryl Townsend was born in Leytonstone, London, England, on July 16, 1991. His father is Troy Townsend. Andros is of Jamaican descent through his father and Cypriot descent through his maternal grandparents, who were born in Limassol. **History**  
Nodes of Ranvier was formed in 2000 in Sioux Falls, South Dakota, by guitarist Jon Parker. The band underwent multiple lineup changes, including Nick Thomas of The Spill Canvas (guitar/backing vocals) and Thomas Hentges of All About Sam (lead vocals). By December 2007, Parker was the only remaining original member. Nodes of Ranvier released their first three albums—including *Lost Senses, More Innocence* (2002)—through Facedown Records before signing with Victory Records in early 2007. Their Victory debut, *Defined By Struggle*, was released in July 2007, but conflicts with the label led to canceled tours and rumors of disbandment. Guitarist Parker confirmed the breakup in late 2007, with their final show occurring on December 31, 2007. The band reunited for Facedown Fest 2017, announced on February 15, 2017, to celebrate Facedown Records' 20th anniversary, featuring Parker, Ryan Knutson (drums/vocals), Kyle Benecke (vocals), Scott McGuire (bass), and Sheldon Swan (guitar).

The music video for "Dirt Room" by Blue October premiered on Yahoo! Music in early 2009 and was directed by Kevin Kerslake. The video features a dramatic narrative where a robber, played by an unidentified actor, is captured and buried alive in an open grave. Surrounding the grave are several onlookers, including members of the band. Blue October is shown performing the song in a dimly lit, industrial setting, adding to the video's intense atmosphere. In a symbolic act, frontman Justin Furstenfeld burns a document displaying the word "lawsuit," reflecting the song's inspiration—a personal betrayal and legal battle Furstenfeld experienced. He has stated that the song was written as a response to someone who attempted to exploit him financially. Furstenfeld initially spoke vaguely about the lawsuit due to legal sensitivities but later revealed its impact on his life and music. The video’s dark themes align with the raw emotion of the track.

The **Tier System (Deprecated)** was a classification framework used by U.S. military planners from circa 2004 to 2011 to categorize unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) by capability and mission role. It did not refer to specific aircraft models but designated operational tiers (e.g., Tier I, II, III) in the overall usage plan. The U.S. Air Force, Army, and Marine Corps each had their own tier systems, which were not fully integrated, reflecting branch-specific priorities and requirements.
"Burma VJ" opened in one theater on May 20, 2009, grossing $16,387 its opening weekend. It later expanded to three theaters and grossed $51,672 by May 2010.

In recent years, the theme park industry has increasingly adopted automated guided vehicles (AGVs) for rides, enhancing both functionality and guest experience. One of the earliest AGV ride systems was introduced in Epcot's *Universe of Energy*, which opened on October 1, 1982. The ride's "Traveling Theatre" used wired navigation to guide vehicles through the attraction. Wired navigation remains common in rides where employees frequently walk over the ride path, such as the now-closed *Great Movie Ride* at Disney's Hollywood Studios. Another example of wired navigation is *The Twilight Zone Tower of Terror* at Hollywood Studios, a combined drop tower and dark ride. In this attraction, the elevator cars function as AGVs, locking into vertical motion cabs for drops and unlocking to drive horizontally between floors, showcasing the versatility of AGV technology in theme park attractions.

On December 12, 2011, Carrier IQ released a 27-page document titled *"Understanding Carrier IQ Technology"* to clarify its software's functions. Crediting Trevor Eckhart, Ashkan Soltani, and Dan Rosenberg, the document provides a technical breakdown of the software's operations on mobile devices. It explains that carrier-supplied "profiles" dictate what data is collected, such as diagnostic metrics and performance logs, which carriers use for service improvement. The document also notes that future updates may modify or discontinue certain features, advising users to monitor changes via RSS feeds.

Heather Dale discovered Medievalism at the age of eighteen while a student at the University of Waterloo through the Society for Creative Anachronism. This led her to begin composing songs inspired by Arthurian legend and other fantasy books. Soon after, she began attending science fiction conventions in Toronto, finding a welcoming community. Dale made her first recordings in 1992 and released her debut album, *The Trial of Lancelot*, in 2000. *Trial* included her most popular song, "Mordred's Lullaby", which had over twenty million views on YouTube as of August 2020.

Nicholas Pieck (also spelled Pick) was born in Gorkum, Holland, on August 29, 1534, to John Pieck and Henrica Clavia, devout Catholics. He attended college at Bois-le-Duc, then joined the Friars Minor at a local convent. Sent to Louvain for priesthood studies, he was ordained in 1558. He devoted himself to apostolic ministry and later became Guardian of the Franciscan friary in his native Gorkum.

Two days after Howell's murder, Oklahoma City police found his stolen GMC Suburban parked in a convenience store parking lot on the city's south side. To determine who left the vehicle there, officers canvassed the area and, acting on a hunch, first visited Kermit Lottie's auto body shop, located four blocks from where the Suburban was recovered. Lottie, a longtime confidential informant, told detectives that Ladell King and at least one other person had attempted to sell him Howell's stolen Suburban the day after the shooting. Lottie recognized the vehicle from news reports and refused to buy it, identifying King as one of the men.

The Starship spacecraft, the upper stage of the SpaceX Starship system, serves as the primary vehicle for transporting payloads and astronauts beyond Earth's atmosphere. Standing at approximately 50 meters tall with a diameter of 9 meters, Starship has a dry mass of around 85 tons. It offers a vast payload volume, dwarfing that of the International Space Station's pressurized volume, and can be extended further to increase its capacity. Starship can be refueled in orbit, allowing for missions to the Moon and Mars, where multiple launches are required to fully refuel the spacecraft. This capability represents a significant advancement in space exploration technology.

In the 1970s, as college athletics grew and programs diverged in resources, the NCAA created a three-division structure (I, II, and III) in 1973 to ensure fair competition. These were renamed in 2006 (per most sources; 2007 per others) as the Football Bowl Subdivision (FBS) and Football Championship Subdivision (FCS), respectively.

After the Seminole Wars, those who remained in Florida adapted to the harsh wetlands environment, maintaining their survival skills honed over decades of warfare. They continued to engage in limited trade with trusted Americans, keeping wary distances from expanding American settlements. During the American Civil War, the Confederate government of Florida offered to provide food and munitions to the Seminole, but the Florida House of Representatives established no formal aid program in 1862. The Seminole remained neutral, as promised aid was never realized due to logistical challenges and Union blockades. In July 1864, General William S. Harney received word that a company of Seminole volunteers had joined the Confederacy, though most Seminole maintained their traditional stance of neutrality. A. McBride, coming to Chattanooga from Eastern Florida State College, brought experience coaching teams that adapted to challenging environments, reflecting the resilience of the Seminole people.

Edith Coates, born on 31 May 1905 in Lincoln, was the daughter of Percy Coates, a commercial traveller and watch repairer, and Eleanor Coates, nee Allen. She attended local schools in Lincoln before winning a scholarship to Trinity College of Music in London, where she studied under Ethel Henry Bird. Afterward, she continued her vocal training with Clive Carey, Dawson Freer, and Dino Borgioli.

Everglades University enrolls 3,072 students annually, with 98% attending full-time. Minorities make up 57% of the student body, primarily Black and Hispanic. The university employs 290 faculty, maintaining an 11:1 student-to-faculty ratio. (43 words)

The voting patterns in **NA-54 Rawalpindi-IV** reveal a competitive constituency with shifting allegiances. In the **2002 elections**, PPP's **Zamarud Khan** won the seat with **31,491 votes (37.6% of the polled votes)**, defeating PML-N's Raja Zafarul Haq by a narrow margin of **2,600 votes**. The 2008 elections saw a similar two-way contest, though exact vote shares are unspecified, with Khan securing victory again. By **2013**, smaller parties gained traction, with their collective vote share rising from **18% in 2017 to 25% in 2019**, reflecting a broader trend toward multi-party politics despite a two-party squeeze. Historically, the constituency has favored tight races, as seen in **1988** when PPP's Raja Shahid Zafar defeated PML-Q's **Basharat Raja** by **16,000 votes**. Raja, a perennial runner-up, lost twice (1985, 1988) before attempting another bid. The historical volatility suggests that alliances between runners-up, like PML-N and PML-Q, could reshape outcomes, given the constituency's narrow margins and shifting voter loyalties.

Harry Davenport married actress Alice Shepard in 1893, and they had one daughter, Dorothy Davenport, who became a prominent actress. The couple divorced in 1896, the same year Harry married actress Phyllis Rankin. Together, Harry and Phyllis had three biological children—Kate, Ned, and Ann—all of whom became actors. Harry also adopted Phyllis's son from a previous marriage, Arthur Rankin, who was the father of Arthur Rankin Jr., co-founder of the Rankin/Bass animation studio. Actress Anne Seymour was Harry Davenport's great-niece through her mother, May Davenport, making her part of the extended Davenport family legacy in the entertainment industry.

Football was introduced to Turkey in the late 19th century by Englishmen living in Salonica, then part of the Ottoman Empire. The first league competition in the country, the Istanbul Football League, was established in the 1904–05 season. Over the following decades, regional leagues emerged, including those in Ankara (1922), Trabzon (1922), Adana (1924), Izmir (1924), Bursa (1924), and Eskisehir (1924). The first national competition, the Turkish Football Championship (Türkiye Futbol Şampiyonası), began in 1924 and continued until 1951. It followed a knockout format, featuring the winners of the top regional leagues. In 1937, the National Division (Millî Küme) was introduced as the first national league competition, bringing together the strongest clubs from the Ankara, Istanbul, and Izmir leagues. This competition lasted until 1950. The modern era of Turkish football began with the creation of the Millî Lig (now known as the Süper Lig) in 1959, marking the establishment of a unified national league.

Following the lifting of the European Union aviation safety ban in 2009, Garuda Indonesia announced an ambitious five-year expansion plan known as the "Quantum Leap." This strategy aimed to transform the airline’s image through a comprehensive overhaul, including new livery, staff uniforms, and a redesigned logo. The plan sought to nearly double the fleet size from 62 to 116 aircraft and increase annual passenger numbers from 10.3 million in 2008 to 27.6 million by 2014. Additionally, Garuda aimed to expand its network to serve 62 domestic and 33 international destinations. As part of this growth, the airline planned to launch a new Jakarta–Amsterdam route in 2010. By 2014, Garuda Indonesia operated non-stop flights to Amsterdam using its Boeing 777-300ER aircraft, offering services five times weekly. This marked a significant milestone in the airline’s efforts to strengthen its presence in Europe and achieve its ambitious growth targets under the Quantum Leap initiative.

The racial composition was 83.5% White (non-Hispanic), 14.1% Black or African American, 0.4% Native American, 0.2% Asian, 0.5% from other races, and 1.3% from two or more races. Additionally, 1.5% of the population identified as Hispanic or Latino of any race.

William Purington Cole Jr. was first elected to the United States House of Representatives in 1926 as a Democrat, serving Maryland's 2nd district. His first term began on March 4, 1927, and ended on March 3, 1929, during the 70th Congress. After losing reelection in 1928, he returned to practicing law. Cole was elected again in 1930, serving from March 4, 1931, until his resignation on October 26, 1942, during his sixth consecutive term. He later pursued a judicial career, leaving Congress to join the United States Customs Court.
