As of the 2010 census, Mackenzie, Missouri had a population of 1,234 residents, with 450 households and 320 families. The population density was 1,200 inhabitants per square mile, and there were 500 housing units, resulting in an average housing unit density of 480 per square mile. The racial composition was 95% White, while Hispanic or Latino individuals of any race made up 3% of the population.

When the Newcastle Arena opened on November 18, 1995, the resident basketball team was the Newcastle Comets, who had been relocated from Sunderland, where they were originally known as the Sunderland Scorpions. In 1996, the Comets were bought by John Hall and were renamed the Newcastle Eagles for the start of the 1996–97 season.

Owen Thomas Jones joined the British Geological Survey in 1900, working in Wales. He became the first professor of geology at Aberystwyth in 1910 and later moved to the University of Manchester in 1919. In 1930, he was appointed Woodwardian Professor of Geology at the University of Cambridge. Jones also served as president of the Geological Society of London twice (1936–1938 and 1950–1951).

In 1942, *Warspite* joined the Eastern Fleet as its flagship under Admiral Sir James Somerville, who had previously commanded her in 1927. Initially based in Ceylon, *Warspite* formed a fast group with the aircraft carriers *Formidable* and *Indomitable*, along with four cruisers and six destroyers. In March, Somerville received intelligence indicating the Japanese Fast Carrier Strike Force was heading towards the Indian Ocean, prompting him to relocate his base to Addu Atoll in the Maldives. The Japanese Fast Carrier Strike Force, led by Vice Admiral Chuichi Nagumo, aimed to destroy the Royal Navy's Eastern Fleet during the Indian Ocean raid in April, utilizing five carriers and four battleships. Despite being outnumbered and outclassed, Somerville hoped to launch a night-time torpedo bomber attack. *Warspite*'s fast group set sail to intercept on 4 April, detecting the Japanese attack on the cruisers *Cornwall* and *Dorsetshire*. The fleets did not meet, and *Warspite* withdrew to Addu Atoll and then to Kilindini on the East African coast to protect convoy routes. The Japanese, however, believed that she was still in Sydney.

Universities in Venezuela have varying degrees of autonomy, though they face significant challenges due to government interference. They primarily receive funds from the Ministry of Education, supplemented by private donations and royalties. Universities must meet strict criteria for funding from external sources. The Minister of Education holds ultimate responsibility and authority over all university activities, including budgeting, inspection, administration, curriculum planning, and infrastructure management. While traditionally autonomous institutions like Universidad Central de Venezuela have resisted governmental control, the Minister can intervene if necessary.

The Cape Ann Museum was founded in 1873 as the Cape Ann Scientific and Literary Association, with Dr. Sarah M. Davidson serving as its first president. In 1923, the Association moved to its permanent headquarters at the Captain Elias Davis House, located at 27 Pleasant Street in Gloucester. During this move, the organization acquired the Davis family collection and, in 1926, adopted the name Cape Ann Historical Association to better reflect its mission. Following the name change, the Association expanded its collections to include art, artifacts, and documents that illustrated the rich history and culture of Cape Ann. In 1937, Alfred Mansfield Brooks became curator and president of the Association, assembling the largest and finest collection of works by Fitz Henry Lane, a renowned 19th-century maritime painter. Under Brooks’ leadership, the museum’s focus on art and history deepened significantly. In 2007, the Board voted to rename the institution the Cape Ann Museum, with the tagline "Art | History | Culture," to more accurately represent its mission and commitment to preserving and presenting the traditions of Cape Ann. Today, the Museum continues to celebrate the region’s art, history, and cultural heritage.

In August 1865, the Australian colony of Victoria received six 35 cwt RBL 40-pounder Armstrong guns for use as mobile coastal fortifications. One gun was fitted to the sloop *Victoria* during 1866–1867, and four were later used as field guns at Hastings. Three guns are known to survive.

María José Ferrada was born in Temuco, Chile, in 1977. She studied social communication and earned a master's degree in Asia-Pacific studies at the University of Santiago, Chile. She currently resides in Santiago.

Henry Vandyke Carter initially began his medical studies in Hull but abandoned them to pursue training as an apothecary in London due to financial constraints. His parents expressed discomfort with his academic and social environment, prompting his transfer to Birmingham to study under John Sawyer. At the age of 17, Carter began taking medical courses at St. George's Hospital in London. In 1848, he matriculated at the University of London, and in 1851, he passed the Intermediate Examination in Medicine with honors, achieving 7th place in Anatomy and Physiology and 5th place in Chemistry. After completing his preclinical studies, Carter traveled to Paris from 1852 to 1853 to further his education and reflect on his specialization.

The New York Times Building was designed by Renzo Piano, marking his first architectural project in New York City. Developed through a partnership between The New York Times Company, Forest City Ratner Companies, and ING Real Estate, the building features interiors designed by Gensler. The main contractor for the core and shell was AMEC Construction Management, Inc., while the contractor for the Times space was Tishman Realty & Construction. Thornton-Tomasetti Group Inc. served as the structural engineer, with the wind consultancy provided by RWDI. The sealant was supplied by Tremco, and the steel was provided by the American Bridge Company. Flack + Kurtz designed the mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems, and the site is officially owned by The New York Times Company.

After the death of Mohammed Toufic al-Assad, the criminal network and its armed contingent were taken over by his son, Hassan al-Assad. Following this transition, the group underwent significant changes; Hassan rebranded the criminal network as a loyalist militia, officially founding the 'Lions of Hussein Brigade' (Liwa Usud al-Hussein) around late June to early July 2015. Aymenn Jawad Al-Tamimi noted that the name "Lions of Hussein" could refer both to the group's leader and to Imam Hussein, symbolizing a dual allegiance. The reorganization aimed to align the militia more closely with the Syrian regime's military objectives, enhancing its legitimacy and operational capacity. By 2017, the Lions of Hussein became part of the larger military structure known as the "Quwat Humat Souriya" (Guardians of Syria Forces). Despite this affiliation, a commander of the Lions of Hussein later denied any formal ties to the Syrian army. Throughout 2015 and 2016, the Lions of Hussein participated in several key offensives, including operations in the Homs desert near Palmyra, the Sahl al-Ghab region, the assault on Marj al-Sultan airbase, and various offensives in north Latakia province against rebel forces.

Göreme's history spans thousands of years, serving as a living space for civilizations like the Hittites, Phrygians, Romans, Byzantines, Seljuks, and Ottomans. Initially a small settlement away from main roads, its isolation may have contributed to its role as a necropolis in Roman times, evidenced by pillared tombs hollowed into fairy chimneys. During the Byzantine era, the area consisted of two adjacent villages—Matiana and Corama (later Korama)—with the modern Open Air Museum located near this historic site. The first written record of these villages dates to the 6th century, referencing St. Hieron, a Christian martyr born in Korama whose severed hand was returned to his mother, as depicted in local churches. Simpler cave churches in the area date from as early as the 4th century, while their famed frescoes span the 9th to 13th centuries. The Council of Chalcedon (451), attended by Pope Leo I's legates and imperial officials, further highlights the region's Christian significance. Göreme later flourished as a monastic center before evolving into today's cultural and historical hub.

Catherine Cox represented Australia at under-21 level from 1995 to 1996, standing out as a player in the 1996 World Youth Netball Championships, where Australia defeated New Zealand in the grand final. She made her senior debut for Australia on June 11, 1997, at the age of 21, against South Africa, with the match ending in a score of 65-38 in Perth.

Hatcham Social formed in late 2006 in New Cross, London, when David Fineberg met brothers Toby and Finnigan Kidd. At the time, Finnigan was a member of Klaxons but left to focus on Hatcham Social. The band released their debut 7-inch single, "Dance as If," on the indie label WaKS Records. Their second single, "Til the Dawn," followed on Loog Records, with its artwork created by British artist Morgan Howell. For this release, 500 limited edition 7-inch sleeves were hand-printed. Hatcham Social received their first national airplay on BBC Radio 6 Music's *Introducing* show, hosted by Tom Robinson, in February 2007.

Route 225 begins at the intersection with Route 27 in the town of Groton, Massachusetts. Upon entering the town of Shirley in Middlesex County, Route 225 is known as Harvard Road. Between its two crossings of the Nashua River, Route 225 is referred to as Shirley Road. The route continues as Long Hill Road after crossing the river for the second time. It turns north onto Route 111, where it runs concurrently with that route. Route 225 and Route 111 follow Main Street until Route 111 diverges at the intersection with the town center. Route 111 diverges from Route 225 in the vicinity of the Shirley center. Finally, Route 225 joins Route 119 in the town of Ayer, completing its connection with this major route.

**Education and community involvement** are critical in combating mosquito-borne diseases, particularly as arboviruses like dengue, Zika, and chikungunya expand into temperate regions due to the spread of *Aedes aegypti* and *Aedes albopictus* mosquitoes. Effective prevention requires integrated strategies, including surveillance, reducing breeding sites, and promoting personal protective measures. Communities are educated on eliminating standing water, using repellents, and recognizing symptoms to curb transmission. Outreach programs identify local risk factors—such as environmental or social gaps—and tailor interventions, like mobile clinics or school partnerships, to address them. By assessing which preventative measures communities are most likely to adopt, programs can enhance compliance. Health workers, local agencies, and schools collaborate in public education campaigns, emphasizing vector control and early detection to mitigate outbreaks.

The promotion of *Encore un soir* began with Celine Dion's Summer Tour 2016, announced on her official website in January 2016. The tour, which ran from 20 June to 31 August 2016, included 28 shows across Canada and France. Several songs from the *Encore un soir* album were performed live for the first time during this tour, including the title track "Encore un soir" and "Les yeux au ciel." On 29 June 2016, Dion performed these songs at the AccorHotels Arena in Paris, marking their live debut. Later, on 24 August 2016, she performed "Encore un soir" and "Les yeux au ciel" for the first time on television during M6's *Music Show – 100% tubes 2016*. Additionally, she appeared on *Le Grand Show* on France 2, which aired in late October 2016, to perform these tracks again. The album's promotion continued into 2017 with the *Celine Dion Live 2017* tour, her first European tour in over eight years, which supported the album and included performances of "Encore un soir." This tour spanned 25 shows across 15 cities in Europe, further solidifying the album's success.

Leigh Diffey, known primarily for his coverage of motorsports, made his Olympic debut at the 2014 Winter Games in Sochi, Russia, as the play-by-play announcer for bobsled, skeleton, and luge for NBC. NBC executive Craig Kowalski expressed interest in expanding Diffey's role beyond motorsports. To prepare, Diffey hosted events like the Rolex 24 At Daytona. Reflecting on his career, Diffey stated, "It's a dream come true to be part of the Olympics, a pinnacle of sports coverage." He covered track and field at the 2016 Summer Olympics in Rio de Janeiro, and bobsled, skeleton, and luge at the 2018 Winter Games in Pyeongchang.

After the October Revolution, Georgy Stepanov supported the Bolshevik political group and continued to serve in the Soviet Navy. He participated in the Ice Cruise of the Baltic Fleet from March to September 1918, which aimed to secure the Baltic Sea against German forces. From September 1918 to March 1919, he commanded the minesweeper "Sheksna." Following this, he held the position of chief of staff until March 1920, when he became the acting commander of the Onega Military Flotilla, engaging in combat against White Army forces on Lake Onega. In May 1920, Stepanov was appointed as the chief of the West Black Sea coast defense sector, serving in this role from June to August 1921. Subsequently, he was the head of the operational management of the Naval Staff from August 1921 to December 1924, and from December 1924 to October 1925, he served as the chief of the Black Sea Fleet.

Mykel Williams began receiving national attention during his high school football career, with 247Sports ranking him as the No. 5 defensive lineman and No. 22 overall recruit in the class of 2022. Rivals.com similarly rated him as a five-star recruit, the No. 2 strongside defensive end, and No. 27 overall. Both 247Sports and Rivals.com highlighted his 'outstanding frame' and 'elite length,' comparing him to Travon Walker. Nolan Smith echoed this comparison, stating, "He's the spitting image of Travon Walker. His arms are long, he moves just as fast, and he's a great athlete."

Bundaberg East State School is a government primary school at 33 Scotland Street, Bundaberg East, Queensland. In 2018, it had 497 students enrolled, with 37 teachers and 23 non-teaching staff. The school caters to Prep to Year 6 and includes a special education program. Its geographical coordinates are approximately -24.8661, 152.3689.

The emblem of Welsberg-Taisten features the red tower of **Welsperg Castle**, representing the historic arms of the **Earls of Welsberg**, whose 12th-century castle northeast of the village gave it its name. Adopted in 1967.

ISG15, a 15-kDa protein, was first identified in 1979 by Peter Lengyel and colleagues and was initially termed the "15-kDa protein induced by interferon (IFN)" due to its molecular weight. In 1987, James E. Darnell and colleagues renamed it "interferon-stimulated gene 15" (ISG15) after identifying its gene as transcriptionally driven by IFN-β. That same year, ISG15 was found to cross-react with anti-ubiquitin antibodies, revealing its structural similarity to ubiquitin. This led to the coining of the term "ISGylation" to describe its ubiquitin-like conjugation to cellular proteins. In subsequent decades, studies highlighted ISG15's critical antiviral activity against viruses such as influenza, herpes, and Sindbis, emphasizing its role in the IFN response. Additionally, ISG15 was discovered to exist extracellularly, secreted by human neutrophils, monocytes, and lymphocytes, despite lacking a signal peptide. It was also detected in human serum samples, suggesting a broader immunomodulatory role beyond its intracellular functions.

The lead single "Save Tonight" debuted at No. 5 on the Swedish singles chart, marking E.M.D.'s sixth top 5 single. It fell to No. 46 the following week but later re-entered the chart, climbing to a new peak of No. 3, showcasing a resurgence in popularity over the subsequent weeks.

"Are We There," Sharon Van Etten's fourth studio album, received widespread critical acclaim upon its release. On Metacritic, the album holds an impressive average score of 87 out of 100, based on 31 reviews, indicating "universal acclaim." Fred Thomas of AllMusic awarded the album 4.5 out of 5 stars, praising its raw emotional depth and Van Etten's ability to communicate profound vulnerability through her music. Will Hermes of *Rolling Stone* gave the album 4 out of 5 stars, likening Van Etten's exploration of romantic decay to the work of Nick Cave, noting that her "darkness contains multitudes." Emily VanDerWerff of *The A.V. Club* also lauded the album, giving it an "A" rating and highlighting its ability to transcend traditional musical structures, describing it as "essential, emotional communication from one person to another." Critics consistently praised Van Etten's commanding vocal performance, intimate songwriting, and the album's ability to balance stark emotional honesty with lush, cathedral-like production, solidifying "Are We There" as a standout in her discography and one of the most compelling releases of its time.

Dinmore Manor, developed starting in 1189, was mostly rebuilt in the 16th century. Around 1850, the house was altered, and an extension was added in 1987. The main house is listed as a building of special architectural interest.

The fluorenone core, particularly when part of a benzofuran ring system, is a basic structural unit in many biologically active natural medicines, attracting significant research attention. Azafluorenones are representative members of this class. Onychine, polyfothine, and isoursuline exhibit antimalarial effects. Cyathocaline intercalates into DNA. Azafluorenone derivatives possess reported biological activities and are used in fluorescence applications, showing topoisomerase I inhibition.

**Migration**  
After Brexit, the UK regained control over immigration from the EU and EEA, ending the principle of free movement, which had allowed EU/EEA citizens to live, work, and study in the UK without restrictions. The *European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018* retained EU law, including free movement rules, as UK law until repealed. The *Immigration and Social Security Co-ordination (EU Withdrawal) Bill* formally ended free movement after the transition period (31 December 2020), paving the way for a points-based system prioritizing skills over nationality. The government’s 2018 white paper proposed a single skills-based system, with a £30,000 salary threshold for skilled workers. EU/EEA citizens already in the UK before 2020 could secure their status via the *EU Settlement Scheme (EUSS)*, launched in 2019, with applications due by June 2021. Irish citizens (including dual British-Irish nationals) were exempt from applying. In a no-deal scenario, EU arrivals before 2021 could have applied for temporary leave to remain until December 2023. The new system aims to align immigration with economic needs while integrating EU migrants already resident under the EUSS.

Impressionism emerged as an artistic style in France in the 1860s, emphasizing loose brushwork, bold colors, and the fleeting effects of light. Major exhibitions of French impressionist works in Boston and New York during the 1880s introduced the style to American audiences. The first exhibit of French impressionist works in New York took place in 1886, presented by the American Art Association and organized by Paul Durand-Ruel. In the late 1880s, American artists like Theodore Robinson and Mary Cassatt adopted the style after visiting France and meeting Claude Monet. Meanwhile, Childe Hassam noticed the growing presence of French impressionist works in American exhibitions.

Susan Catherine Reed was born on January 11, 1926, in Columbia, South Carolina, to Daniel Reed, an actor and film director, and Isadora Bennett, a theater publicist. The family’s liberal-to-communist home was a gathering place for folk luminaries like Carl Sandburg and Leadbelly, while visiting actors from Dublin’s Abbey Theatre, including Ralph Cullinan and Farrell Pelley, nurtured her love of Irish folk music. The Reeds later moved to New York, where Susan and her older brother Jared, also a folk singer, grew up. Reed played harp and zither at private parties and for wounded soldiers, though she initially aspired to be an actress. Discovered by talent scout John Hammond, she debuted at Café Society in Greenwich Village.

In 1770, Henry Strachey married Jane (Kelsall) Latham (1738-1824), the widow of Captain Thomas Latham (Context 3). They had five children: Charlotte-Margaret, Henry, Edward (father of Sir Edward Strachey, 3rd Baronet and Richard Strachey), Richard, and Lucy (Context 3, 7, 6). Sir Edward Strachey, 3rd Baronet, had six sons (Context 7). Sir Richard Strachey married Jane Maria Grant, who became Lady Strachey, and they had thirteen children (Context 8). Lady Strachey died in 1928 (Context 8).

Sally Kader is recognized for her extensive involvement in global peace-building, particularly through interfaith dialogue and promoting awareness of issues such as human rights, racial discrimination, and the transatlantic slave trade. Much of her work is committed to fostering peace and sustainable development. Kader has hosted high-profile interfaith dialogues between Muslim, Christian, and Jewish leaders, and moderated dialogues between Indian and Pakistani communities. As President of the US Federation for Peace in the Middle East (USFMEP) and co-founder of the International Federation for Peace and Sustainable Development (IFPSD), she promotes values of peace, human rights, and cultural heritage.

In the early 20th century, Hungarian immigration to New Zealand began with István Rácz arriving in Tuatapere, Southland, in 1909. He wrote letters to his home in Csongrád, Hungary, prompting the Szivák, Kollát, and Kókay families to join him in 1911. Over the next two decades, approximately 100 more families followed. By 1970, one of István Rácz's descendants, Stephen Kokay, was chosen as the representative of the Waiau district on the Wallace County Council. His daughter, Dr. Ilona Kokay, teaches biology at the University of Otago. Internationally, Les Kokay is recognized for his writings on Bob Dylan, while Mike Racz holds the Guinness record for the fastest oyster opener.

In 1978, the Minnesota Fillies hired Julia Yeater as head coach after her two-season tenure leading Western Kentucky University's women's basketball team. Yeater, who held a master's degree from Indiana State, joined the Fillies partly due to her desire to advance opportunities for female coaches in the Women's Basketball League (WBL). She replaced Billie Moore, inheriting a team facing roster challenges, including an injury to center Trish Roberts.

Since 2005, fertilizer subsidies have gained renewed attention as a potent tool for wide-scale development in Africa, particularly highlighted by Malawi's recent success in tripling grain yields. Jeffrey Sachs, founder of the Millennium Promise Alliance, emphasizes that governments should provide subsistence farmers with access to chemical fertilizers, high-yield seed varieties, and modern irrigation to enhance productivity.

In April 1978, the communist People's Democratic Party of Afghanistan (PDPA) seized power in Afghanistan in the Saur Revolution. Within months, opponents of the communist government launched an uprising in eastern Afghanistan that quickly expanded into a civil war waged by guerrilla mujahideen against government forces countrywide. The Islamic Unity of Afghanistan Mujahideen insurgents received military training and weapons in neighboring Pakistan and China. The Soviet Union sent thousands of military advisers to support the PDPA government. Increasing friction between the more radical "Khalq" and the "Parcham" factions further destabilized the country. By July 1979, the United States, whose president Jimmy Carter was focused on Iran, initiated a covert program through the CIA to financially support the mujahideen.

The 5 Year MD Program at All Saints University School of Medicine is designed for recent high school graduates. It includes 4 semesters of premedical courses, covering essential subjects before transitioning to the Basic Medical Science portion of the 4-Year MD program, allowing students to continue their medical education seamlessly.

"Gloriously Bright" was published in the January 1991 issue of *Analog Science Fiction and Fact*. Parts of the story were later reprinted as chapters 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 of *Xenocide*. It also includes 20 paragraphs recounting Jane's story from *Speaker for the Dead*, which are not republished elsewhere.

In the summer of 1923, a wildcat strike in the Berlin printing industry was triggered by the refusal of management to hire additional workers for the new printing presses at the Reichsbank's Reich printing plant. The Kommunistische Partei Deutschlands (KPD) instigated the strike, which led to the banknote presses stopping. This disruption alarmed the government, prompting a response. Other workers, including railway employees and civil servants, soon joined the strike, with the primary demand being higher wages and improved working conditions.

Sleepy Sun was originally formed in 2005 under the name Mania by students at the University of California, Santa Cruz. The original lineup consisted of Bret Constantino (vocals), Matt Holliman, Evan Reiss (guitars), Brian Tice (drums), and Hubert Guy (bass). Constantino, Holliman, and Reiss were among those who lived on the same floor during their freshman year. In 2007, the band changed their name to Sleepy Sun and welcomed former singer Rachel Fannan as a new member. Their debut album, titled "Embrace," was self-released in 2008 through their own record label and publishing company, Sol Diamond. Following the album's release, Hubert Guy left the band, and Jack Allen replaced him as the bass player. "Embrace" was later re-issued by ATP Recordings in May 2009. In March 2010, Sleepy Sun opened for the Arctic Monkeys during their U.S. tour, which helped elevate their profile and showcase their unique blend of garage rock and psychedelia.

Christy Jenkins, introduced in the sixth episode of *Charmed*'s eighth season, was developed as a character shrouded in mystery and trauma. Glen A. Kern described her as potentially suffering from Stockholm Syndrome after spending 15 years with demons following her kidnapping at a young age. This event, and whether she was inherently evil or simply a product of her upbringing, became a point of contention within the fandom. Kern acknowledged this debate, stating that the question of Christy's true nature was a key factor in her character development. Kern expressed a desire to resolve this question creatively in the series finale.

**Con†Stellation XXIX: Leo** was the 29th convention in the Con†Stellation series, held from February 7–9, 2025, in Huntsville, Alabama. The event featured **Wen Spencer** as Guest of Honor, **Vincent di Fate** as Artist Guest of Honor, and **Warren Buff** as Fan Guest of Honor. **Phil Reed** served as Master of Ceremonies, while the musical group **The Moon and Leo** made a special appearance. The convention celebrated the constellation Leo, coinciding with its prominence in the night sky and the Moon's passage through it on February 11, 2025.

Jennifer Paige's debut single, "Crush," written by Andy Goldmark and Mark Mueller, became a breakout hit in 1998 after Los Angeles-based KIIS-FM first played it. The song quickly became the station's most requested track that summer, despite lacking standard label promotion. In the U.S., "Crush" peaked at No. 3 on the *Billboard* Hot 100 and spent eight weeks in the Top 10. Internationally, it reached No. 1 in Australia, Canada, and New Zealand, solidifying its status as a global pop sensation.

Despite trade rumors during the 2013 offseason, LaMarcus Aldridge expressed a desire to remain with the Portland Trail Blazers, while also candidly requesting improvements to the team's roster. Aldridge started his eighth NBA season strongly, recording five consecutive double-doubles from November 9 to 17. On November 23, 2013, during a game against the Golden State Warriors, Aldridge was involved in an altercation after Warriors center Andrew Bogut became entangled with Joel Freeland. While context 1 does not mention any consequences for Aldridge after the altercation on November 23, 2013, context 1 does mention that Mo Williams was suspended for one game without pay and that LaMarcus Aldridge and Wesley Matthews have been fined for their roles in a Saturday night scrum with the Golden State Warriors. On December 12, 2013, Aldridge achieved a significant milestone, scoring 31 points and grabbing a career-high 25 rebounds in a 111-104 victory over the Houston Rockets, becoming the first player in Trail Blazers history to record at least 30 points and 25 rebounds in a single game. On January 23, 2014, Aldridge recorded a career-high 44 points, along with 13 rebounds, 5 assists, and 2 blocks in a 110-105 victory over the Denver Nuggets.

After the Second World War, No. 617 Squadron RAF replaced its Avro Lancasters with Avro Lincolns before transitioning to the English Electric Canberra jet bomber in 1952. Deployed to Malaya for four months in 1955, the squadron returned to RAF Binbrook and disbanded on 15 December 1955. Reformed at RAF Scampton on 1 May 1958 as part of RAF Bomber Command's V-bomber force, it maintained the UK's strategic nuclear deterrent. Equipped with the Avro Vulcan B1 from August 1960, its aircraft were upgraded to the Vulcan B1A with an electronic countermeasures tailpod by 23 May 1961. The squadron's role was high-level strategic bombing, armed with free-fall nuclear weapons, including British designs like Yellow Sun and, briefly, American bombs under Project E arrangements.

In the Netherlands, "Happy" reached No. 1 on the Dutch Top 40 chart in October 2013, a month before its official single release. This was partially due to airplay from radio station 3FM, online downloads, and its feature in a Transavia commercial. "Happy" was released worldwide on November 21, 2013. In 2014, after spending 35 weeks on the chart and still in the top 15, "Happy" became the most successful song in the Dutch Top 40 of all time.

CD38 was first identified as a surface marker of thymus cell lymphocytes in 1980. It was later described as a marker on B cells, monocytes, and natural killer (NK) cells in 1999. Around this time, CD38 was also discovered to have enzymatic activity, specifically as a nicotinamide dinucleotide (NAD+) catabolic enzyme. This activity is capable of synthesizing cyclic adenosine diphosphate ribose (cADPR) and other calcium-releasing second messengers, including nicotinic acid adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NAADP).

At the medieval University of Oxford, students organized themselves into two nations: the Australes and the Boreales. The Australes, representing southern England, included scholars from the Romance lands and the Celtic Fringe, such as the Welsh. The Boreales primarily came from northern England. The Australes were the more powerful group, reflecting the dominance of southern England during the period.

USS Concord (CL-10) began her maiden cruise on 23 November 1923 and ended on 9 April 1924. During this voyage, she visited Mediterranean ports, passed through the Suez Canal, and rounded the Cape of Good Hope. After exercising with the fleet in the Caribbean, she returned to Philadelphia. As the flagship of Commander, Destroyer Squadrons, Scouting Fleet, Concord cruised the Caribbean and sailed through the Panama Canal to exercise in the Hawaiian Islands in 1924 and 1925. She participated in the Presidential Fleet Review conducted by Calvin Coolidge on 4 June 1927.

Limiting is a form of dynamic range compression with a high ratio, typically above 4:1, often reaching infinity:1. While both processes reduce dynamic range, limiting focuses on preventing peak levels from exceeding a threshold, ensuring signals remain within safe boundaries without significantly altering the sound.
The Socorro Consolidated School District serves approximately 1,334 students and has one high school, Socorro High School.

"An Eakins Masterpiece Restored: Seeing The Gross Clinic Anew" opened at the Philadelphia Museum of Art on July 24, 2010, and ran through January 9, 2011. The exhibition celebrated the meticulous conservation of Thomas Eakins' 1875 masterpiece, *The Gross Clinic*, and explored its historical and artistic significance. The theme, "Then and Now," was inspired by Dr. Samuel D. Gross's nearly two-hour-long lecture titled "A System of Surgery," which highlighted advancements in medical practice during his time. The first gallery of the exhibition was styled to evoke the Centennial Exhibition of 1876, where *The Gross Clinic* debuted, and included photographs, ephemera, and interior shots of the original exhibition sites. The second gallery delved into the painting’s conservation, showcasing the detective work of conservators who uncovered how the artwork had changed over time and restored it to its original appearance. The final gallery presented a video and other materials that detailed the restoration process, offering visitors a behind-the-scenes look at the techniques and decisions involved in preserving Eakins' vision. Together, the exhibition provided a comprehensive narrative of the painting’s creation, historical reception, and the efforts to ensure its legacy as one of the most celebrated works of 19th-century American art.

Many Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) models over-prioritize current high-value customers, often at the expense of potential customers. This stems from a mathematical issue where models focus heavily on aggregate metrics, masking inaccuracies at the individual level. While high-value customers are valuable due to their frequent purchases, brand loyalty, and lower service and communication costs, they can reach saturation, limiting growth opportunities. Effective marketing can elevate low-value customers by addressing their needs and increasing engagement, but many CLV models fail to account for this potential. Additionally, these models often neglect the importance of gathering insights from potential customers, which can be achieved through targeted surveys or feedback mechanisms. This approach helps businesses identify untapped opportunities and balance short-term gains with long-term customer value.

The shell of *Odostomia farallonensis* is small, ovate, and subconic, with a smooth, glossy surface and a white coloration. It typically measures about 2.5 mm in length. The protoconch consists of two whorls that are deeply immersed, with only the edge of the last whorl visible, giving a tilted appearance. The teleoconch comprises four whorls, which are slightly rounded and exhibit a well-defined suture. The summit of the shell is rounded and not sharply pointed. The aperture is oval in shape, with a thin outer lip. The columella is slender and features a single, weakly developed fold near its insertion, characteristic of the genus *Odostomia*.

Schwann cells play a crucial role in Wallerian degeneration by proliferating and phagocytizing myelin debris following peripheral nerve injury. In addition to myelin phagocytosis, they are involved in the degradation of their own myelin and the removal of extracellular debris. The phagocytic activity of Schwann cells has been investigated through electron microscopy in various degenerating nerve segments. MAC-2, a galectin-3 protein, is expressed in Schwann cells and is specific for binding galactose. Its expression has been observed in both macrophage-rich and Schwann-rich degenerating nerves. A strong correlation exists between MAC-2 levels and the extent of myelin phagocytosis, with deficiencies in MAC-2 leading to impaired myelin removal during degeneration.

Association methods in protein–protein interaction (PPI) prediction analyze sequence-signatures to distinguish interacting from non-interacting pairs by identifying conserved patterns in protein domains. Classifiers are trained using association rule mining (ARM), which generates rules linking interaction site properties to PPI types. These methods evaluate domain interactions using log-odds scores, calculated as log(Pij / (Pi × Pj)), where Pij is the probability of two domains interacting, and Pi and Pj are the probabilities of individual domains. Predicted interactions are characterized by single interaction events at the domain level, with expectation scores used to assess potential interactions. However, a limitation of association methods is their reliance on sufficient domain interaction data, excluding infrequently observed domains, and the low coverage of transient interactions due to limited structural data in protein databases like the Protein Data Bank (PDB).

Located near the Uttar Pradesh border in northeastern Madhya Pradesh, Jaitwara is a small town close to Satna, its nearest city.

Football is the most popular sport in Ta'if, with Wej SC representing the city in the Saudi Second Division. Wej SC plays its games at King Fahd Sports City, located near as-Sayl as-Saghir. Expatriates have introduced cricket, badminton, and other sports, with cricket particularly popular among South Asian communities. The city also hosts regional football tournaments, such as the Under-13 Regional Teams Championship.

Trichuris muris infection in mice induces a Th1 immune response, epithelial dysregulation, and inflammatory cytokine upregulation. Heavy infection with Platynosomum fastosum in cats leads to bile duct hyperplasia, fibrosis, and potentially hepatic failure. Cryptosporidium parvum infection in mice causes intestinal inflammation and dysregulation of the intestinal epithelium.

The area was first settled by Europeans around 1643 and became part of the town of Hempstead. Until 18 July 2020, Dnipriany belonged to the Nova Kakhovka Municipality, which was abolished during Ukraine's administrative reform aimed at reducing the number of raions. After the reform, Kherson Oblast had five raions, and Dnipriany was merged into the newly established Kakhovka Raion.

In 1958, a second major incident occurred at the National Research Universal (NRU) reactor at Chalk River Laboratories. This event involved the overheating and rupture of a fuel rod, which caught fire as it was being removed by a robotic crane. When the crane arm moved away from the reactor vessel, a three-foot length of burning uranium fuel broke off and fell into a shallow maintenance pit. The resulting fire posed significant challenges, as attempts to reinsert or remove the rod failed, leading to further complications. Despite spraying with water, the fire continued. Eventually, the fire was extinguished, and the incident required extensive cleanup efforts involving both AECL personnel and the Canadian military.

According to the city's website, Piła's name derives from the Polish word for "saw," referring to a water-powered sawmill. The Mongol invasion of 1241, ending with the Battle of Legnica, devastated Poland and may have contributed to German colonization. German immigration diminished during the reign of King Casimir IV Jagiellon (1447-1492), under whom Poland defeated the Teutonic Order in 1466. Piła is located in the flat plains of Poland.

Before 1941, Belgian workers could volunteer to work in Germany, with approximately 189,000 signing up by spring 1941. Among them, some joined the Organisation Todt (OT), responsible for large construction projects like the Atlantic Wall, while others joined the Nationalsozialistisches Kraftfahrkorps (NSKK). Compulsory deportation of Belgian workers began in October 1942, with firms initially required to select a percentage of their workforce for deportation. By 1943, entire age classes of men (18–50) and unmarried women (18–35) were conscripted. In total, around 375,000 Belgians were sent to Germany during the war, performing jobs primarily in heavy industry and agriculture under harsh conditions, including inadequate food, clothing, and shelter. Many were housed in overcrowded camps and faced long working hours. Additionally, workers in German towns were at risk of Allied bombing, further endangering their lives. The forced labor system exemplified the extreme exploitation of the Belgian population under German occupation.

The flamelet concept models a turbulent flame as an ensemble of thin, laminar, locally one-dimensional flamelet structures embedded within the turbulent flow field. A common laminar flame used to represent a flamelet in turbulent flows is the counterflow diffusion flame, characterized by opposed, axisymmetric fuel and oxidizer jets. As the distance between the jets decreases or the jet velocities increase, the flame experiences strain, departs from chemical equilibrium, and may eventually extinguish. In laminar counterflow diffusion flame experiments, the species mass fraction and temperature fields can be measured or calculated. When calculated, a self-similar solution exists, allowing the governing equations to be simplified to one dimension along the axis of the fuel and oxidizer jets. This simplification enables affordable complex chemistry calculations along the stagnation point streamline, making the counterflow diffusion flame a valuable tool for studying flamelet behavior in turbulent combustion.

**Anas el-Fiqqi** served as Egypt's **Minister of Information** from 2004 until his resignation on **February 12, 2011**, amid the **2011 Egyptian revolution**. Previously, he was chairman of the **Radio and Television Union**. During the uprising, he was implicated in suppressing dissent, including the **closure of Al Jazeera's Cairo bureau**, and falsely stating on state TV that Hosni Mubarak would not resign. After stepping down, he faced charges of **squandering $1.88 million** of public funds, allegedly spent on **campaigning for the National Democratic Party (NDP)**. On **February 19, 2011**, his detention was renewed for further investigation. He was later referred to the **Cairo Criminal Court** for financial misconduct.

The seven constituent districts sent their students to Bridgeton High School before the Cumberland Regional High School was established. In the 1970s, Bridgeton High School faced racial tensions and overcrowding issues. The initial school board for the new high school was selected on December 10, 1974, by the county superintendent. Construction was expected to take about 48 months. Residents approved the referendum by a margin of 1,306 to 1,295, authorizing a $10.2 million bond referendum to address overcrowding and provide a better educational environment.

A Spanish version of "2 Become 1," titled "Seremos 1 los 2," was included as a B-side to the American single release. Later pressings of the *Spice* album in Spain, Chile, Colombia, and Mexico also featured the Spanish version. The track is almost entirely translated, maintaining the original vocal arrangement except for Victoria's original verse. In 1989, Paul Lowden, head of Sahara Casino Partners Limited (later renamed Santa Fe Gaming Corporation), announced plans to build a hotel-casino in the sparsely populated northwest area of Las Vegas. At the time, the region was largely undeveloped, and many considered the project a risky venture. The Santa Fe hotel-casino, built with a southwestern theme, was constructed at a cost of $53 million on a 40-acre site located approximately nine miles north of the Las Vegas Strip. Despite initial opposition from local residents, who feared the development would disrupt their rural lifestyle, the project moved forward, and the Santa Fe received its gaming license in late 1990. The property officially opened in 1991, featuring amenities such as a 200-room hotel, a 65,000-square-foot casino, a bowling center, and an ice skating arena, catering primarily to local residents in the growing area.

Andhra Pradesh boasts beautiful beaches such as Rishikonda and Mypadu, alongside significant caves like the Undavalli Caves, known for Indian rock-cut architecture, and the Belum Caves, the second longest in India. Tourist attractions also include the serene Araku Valley and the picturesque Horsley Hills. Notably, Arma Konda, the highest peak in Andhra Pradesh, is located in the Eastern Ghats.

U.S. District Attorney Francis Heney charged Senator Mekiel Letrel Mitchell with illegally using his position to aid a client in fraudulent land patent claims. Testifying against Mitchell were his law partner, Judge Albert H. Tanner, and personal secretary, Harry C. Robertson. **Error management theory and the smoke detector principle**  
Error management theory (EMT), a psychological framework, suggests that under uncertainty, cognitive adaptations minimize the cost of errors rather than maximizing accuracy. Proposed by Martie Haselton and David Buss, EMT posits that judgments about opportunities and threats err toward minimizing the most costly errors—false negatives (missing threats) or false positives (overreacting). This is analogous to smoke detectors, which are designed to be overly sensitive to ensure early fire detection, even if this results in occasional false alarms. Just as overly sensitive smoke detectors prioritize safety over precision, EMT suggests threat-mitigating adaptations may produce "better safe than sorry" biases, such as prejudice, to avoid catastrophic outcomes. However, excessive sensitivity can lead to unnecessary disruptions, mirroring how overly cautious judgments may fuel unwarranted biases. The smoke detector principle highlights that adaptations often favor caution over accuracy.

Bandy arrived in the United States in its informal manner through Irish immigrants in the 19th century. Elements of bandy were absorbed into the emerging sport of ice hockey, particularly in the northeastern states. While ice hockey was rapidly growing and organizing in cities like Boston and New York, bandy began to organize in regions with significant Scandinavian populations, such as Minnesota and Wisconsin, around the turn of the 20th century.

The 2005 revisions to the International Health Regulations (IHR) aimed to enhance global capacity to detect and respond to public health emergencies while minimizing disruptions to travel and trade. However, the WHO's delayed and inadequate response to the 2014-2016 West African Ebola epidemic and the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted significant shortcomings. By 2015, 127 of 196 countries failed to meet the eight core public health capacities, including reporting potential Public Health Emergencies of International Concern (PHEICs). High-level panels have since criticized the IHR's implementation and effectiveness in coordinating global health responses.

The Whanganui District Health Board serves an area with a population that increased by 213,453 people (5.9%) since the 2013 census, and by 570,957 people (17.6%) since the 2006 census. Dorothy Paget, born in 1905 to a mother from a wealthy family, was known for her eccentric personality and significant contributions to horse racing. She owned Ballymacoll Stud, a renowned breeding farm in Co. Meath, Ireland, and had a stable that included over 1,500 race victories. Dorothy Paget was the British flat racing Champion Owner in 1943 and led the National Hunt ownership rankings in 1933-34, 1940-41, and 1951-52. She was also a leading owner of the Cheltenham Gold Cup, with seven winners, including the most successful horse ever, Golden Miller, who won five consecutive times from 1932 to 1936. Dorothy Paget's horses also secured the Champion Hurdle twice, with Insurance in 1932 and 1933, Solford in 1940, and Distel in 1946. Golden Miller was the only horse to win both the Cheltenham Gold Cup and The Derby in the same season, achieving this feat in 1934.

In 2018–2019, Felix Kjellberg (PewDiePie) engaged in a highly publicized subscriber competition with Indian music label T-Series. On October 5, 2018, Kjellberg uploaded a diss track titled *"Bitch Lasagna"* (later renamed *"Smash PewDiePie"*), which replaced *"A Funny Montage"* as his most-viewed video. The track, part of his "Subscribe to PewDiePie" campaign, accused T-Series of using bots to inflate their subscriber count, though YouTube's routine audits found no evidence of this. Media outlets criticized the track for containing anti-Indian slurs and stereotypes, which a court later deemed "abusive, vulgar, and racist." Kjellberg expressed concern that a corporate channel surpassing him would harm YouTube's platform culture. Online campaigns boosted his subscriber growth, with his channel gaining 6.62 million subscribers in December 2018 alone—nearly matching the 7 million gained in all of 2017. Despite the controversy, the competition fueled record viewership, with his channel amassing over 500 million views that December.

Ferdinand-François-Auguste Donnet (born November 16, 1795, in Bourg-Argental; died December 1882, in Bordeaux) was a French Roman Catholic Cardinal. Educated at the College of Annonay and Lyon's St. Irenaeus Seminary, he was ordained in 1819. Before his appointment as Coadjutor Bishop of Nancy and Titular Bishop of Rhosus in 1835, his earlier positions included professor, assistant priest and mission preacher in Lyon and in Tours. As Archbishop of Bordeaux from 1837, his ecclesiastical province corresponded with Guyenne and included Navarre. He was known for his energy in publishing and church restoration. Donnet forcefully argued for the canonization of Saint Emilie de Rodat. He was a major figure during the height of Napoleon III's power. Pope Pius IX named him Cardinal in 1853 and he participated in the conclave of 1878. Twelve volumes of his pastoral instructions and writings were published posthumously.

Alberto Carlos Taquini joined the renowned research team led by **Bernardo Houssay** (Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1946) at the **Department of Physiology** of the University of Buenos Aires School of Medicine. He collaborated with **Luis Federico Leloir** (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 1970) and worked alongside **Eduardo Braun-Menéndez** and **Juan Carlos Fasciolo**, who co-discovered **angiotensin** in 1939. The team demonstrated the enzymatic nature of the **renin-angiotensin system (RAS)**, linking it to hypertension. RAS plays a critical role in regulating blood pressure, cardiovascular function, and fluid balance, with dysregulation implicated in conditions like heart failure and kidney disease. Taquini became head of the Department of Physiology in **1961**, continuing the team's pioneering work.

Eonyang Amethyst Cave, once an abandoned amethyst mine, has been transformed into Amethyst Cavern Park, the largest man-made cave tourist attraction in Korea. Visitors can explore themed halls such as the Cave of Wish and Amethyst Energy Room on foot, and enjoy a boat ride through the cave's river. Outside the cave, there are amusement park rides, souvenir stores, and food stalls. The park features exhibits and dioramas depicting the history and significance of amethyst, including the legacy of Neanderthal dioramas. With an average temperature of 12-14°C year-round and a length of 2.5 kilometers, the cave offers a unique experience. Rare amethyst specimens and geodes can also be viewed within the cave.

Alan is a masculine given name in the English language. The name is believed to have been brought to England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror in the 11th century. From England, the name spread to Ireland. In Ireland and Scotland, Alan is associated with the Gaelic *ailin*, a diminutive of *ail*, meaning "rock".

Correlated color temperature (CCT) simplifies the description of the perceived "warmness" or "coolness" of a white light source. Measured in Kelvin (K), CCT approximates the color temperature of a black body radiator whose color most closely resembles the light source. Higher CCT values indicate a cooler, bluer light, while lower values indicate a warmer, more reddish light. For example, daylight is around 5600K and appears blue, while tungsten light is around 3200K and appears golden. Although the Kelvin scale measures the light source's color, light sources with the same CCT may still appear different due to variations in their spectral power distribution. Fluorescent lights, while inexpensive, often differ from ideal black body radiators. Some fluorescent lamps are designed with high faithfulness to an ideal light.

Eureka Township is represented in the Sac County Board of Supervisors by a member from the Republican Party. In the Iowa Senate, Craig Williams, also a Republican, represents District 4. Gary Worthan, a Republican, represents District 11 in the Iowa House of Representatives, while Randy Feenstra, a Republican, represents Iowa's 4th Congressional District in the U.S. House.

The United States played a key role in the 1964 Brazilian coup. Ambassador Lincoln Gordon later admitted that the U.S. embassy had funded anti-Goulart candidates in the 1962 municipal elections. The embassy also encouraged plotters opposing President João Goulart, with Gordon sending a 1963 document to CIA director John McCone and top U.S. officials outlining strategies to overthrow him. During the coup, the U.S. positioned Navy oil tankers from Aruba and the USS Forrestal carrier task force off Rio de Janeiro under Operation Brother Sam, with extra military and intelligence personnel aboard, to provide covert support and deter resistance.

Prejudice is often colloquially associated with irrational hostility towards certain groups, while discrimination refers to adverse differential treatment based on these prejudices. An evolutionary perspective does not justify the presence of discrimination but views it as a byproduct of mechanisms designed to enhance reproductive success. Status within a group, crucial for mating opportunities, is an integral part of group living from an evolutionary standpoint. Indiscriminate cooperation is inherently unstable because it fails to recognize and punish free riders, who benefit from the cooperation without contributing. In areas like pathogen avoidance and intergroup conflict, a lack of discrimination can lead to increased disease transmission and conflict resolution failures. Evolutionarily, those who can discriminate more effectively to respond to the specific affordances of others generally have higher fitness.

The fourth season of *Lost* was produced by ABC Studios and Touchstone Television. Damon Lindelof and Carlton Cuse served as showrunners, with post-production completed in Los Angeles. Executive producers included Bryan Burk, Jack Bender, Jeff Pinkner, and Stephen Williams. The staff writers consisted of Jeffrey Lieber, J.J. Abrams, Damon Lindelof, and Carlton Cuse. The supervising producer for the season was Stephen Williams, while regular directors included Jack Bender and Stephen Williams, among others. The season was primarily filmed on location in Hawaii.

Nishat Majumdar was born on January 5, 1981, into the Majumdar family in Ramganj, Noakhali District (now in Lakshmipur District), Bangladesh. Her father, Abdul Mannan Majumdar, was a businessman. Nishat was the second of four children born to Abdul Mannan and Ashura Majumdar. She received bachelor's and master's degrees in accounting from Dhaka City College and is married to Rafikul Islam.

The Grodzka Gate – NN Theatre has been implementing the "Historia Mówiona" (Oral History) project since 1998, focusing on preserving the memories of Lublin's residents, particularly regarding the city's pre-World War II history. To date, the project has collected testimonies from over 800 individuals, amounting to more than 3,000 hours of audio and video recordings. These interviews primarily explore life in Lublin and its surroundings before the war, with a focus on Jewish cultural heritage. The materials, significant for documenting Poland's historical memory, are available online and organized thematically. Using the dLibra system, the files comply with international metadata standards, including Europeana, ensuring accessibility and integration with global digital archives. This extensive archive is among the ten largest digital libraries in Poland, contributing to the preservation and dissemination of cultural heritage.

William W. Arnold married Mary Evelina Shaw on July 14, 1863, in Wrentham, Massachusetts. Their son Clarence, born around 1869, became a physician. Another son, John, died by 1913. A granddaughter was named Urana.

Romancing SaGa 3 takes place in a world where the Rise of Morastrum, an event that occurs every 300 years, causes all beings born during this period to perish, except for one survivor known as the Child of Destiny. Six hundred years prior to the game's events, this role was filled by the Archfiend, while 300 years ago, it was the Matriarch who emerged. Prior to the game's opening, the world prepares for the upcoming Rise of Morastrum, with eight main characters—each with their unique backstory—hunting for the Child of Destiny. Although they begin their journeys separately, these protagonists eventually cross paths, uniting their efforts to prevent the event or guide the Child of Destiny towards their destined path.

Dr. Lee Iu-cheung, born in Hong Kong to a family originating from Xinhui, Guangdong, spent his childhood in the Central and Western District. After excelling in his Cambridge University local examinations, he was awarded the prestigious Lee Iu Cheung Engineering Bursary. In 1913, he joined the Faculty of Engineering at the University of Hong Kong. In 1919, he pursued a special study on River Conservancy and Sanitary Engineering at Cornell University but had to abandon his studies due to his father’s passing. Upon returning to Hong Kong, he became a lecturer at the University of Hong Kong, contributing significantly to the institution’s development.

The Goliath stick insect has evolved a form of passive camouflage and plant mimicry, resembling the leaves or branches of trees through its coloration and shape. Its swaying body movement mimics the natural motion of leaves and branches, aiding in avoiding detection by predators and enhancing its survival in the wild.

In the essay "The Construction of Identity in Art: Alberto Rey's Journey," the construction of identity in art is highlighted by the author as a critical theme in Rey's work. For over a decade, Rey's artistic endeavors focused on reclaiming his Cuban heritage, a process marked by concerns about cultural dislocation and the loss of identity. During this period, he painted various works that analyzed his values and experiences, often reflecting on his childhood spent in Miami after fleeing Cuba. Since creating these beloved memories, Rey's work has sought to mend the fragmented narratives of his past. The Appropriated Memories Series (1996-1997) aims to explore and reinterpret personal and collective memories, using imagery that represents both his cultural roots and the complexities of identity. The images in this series serve as a visual dialogue, bridging the gap between memory and identity, while addressing broader themes of cultural diversity and environmental awareness.

Katanga gained control of the mineral-rich Congo Pedicle as a result of colonial negotiations, though the main copper orebody was geographically divided between Katanga and Northern Rhodesia by the watershed. The loss of Garanganza to the *Compagnie du Katanga* (CFS) meant that Northern Rhodesia lost out on the Congolese Copperbelt. While Alfred Sharpe, then governor of Nyasaland, had failed to secure Msiri's kingdom through negotiation, the Brethren missionaries, including Dan Crawford, maintained a strict policy of neutrality, refusing political involvement. Any chance of the British South Africa Company (BSAC) securing Garanganza ended when Msiri was killed by Omer Bodson, second-in-command of the CFS expedition led by Canadian Captain William Grant Stairs. Some British in Northern Rhodesia viewed Stairs as a mercenary and traitor for his role in securing Katanga for the Belgians.

Robert Burns, a Scottish poet, achieved remarkable popularity in Russia, becoming known as a 'people's poet'. This resonated deeply, particularly given the historical context of Imperial Russia, where a vast divide existed between the often-illiterate peasantry and the wealthy aristocracy. Burns's translated works symbolized inspiration and spoke to the lives of ordinary Russians. In Soviet Russia, Burns was further elevated, with some composers potentially viewing his radical views as a subtle way to express their own non-conformist ideas. A new translation of his poems in 1924 sold over 600,000 copies, highlighting his enduring appeal. The Soviet Union was also the first country to honor Burns with a commemorative stamp in 1956. His poetry continues to be taught in Russian schools alongside national poets. Burns was an admirer of those who broke free from societal constraints and held contempt for the aristocracy's pretensions. The Soviet state used Burns' work to promote an image of a poet of the people.

On 2 December 2017, the local iwi Te Kawerau ā Maki placed an unofficial rāhui over the kauri forest in the Waitākere Ranges to prevent the spread of kauri dieback disease. The rāhui aimed to protect the kauri trees and, by extension, the health of the entire forest. Initially, the public frequently breached the rāhui, leaving the Waitākere Ranges as one of the few large forested areas still accessible to the public in West Auckland. On 20 February 2018, Auckland Council announced the closure of all forested areas in the Waitākere Ranges to the public due to the ineffectiveness of the rāhui, also closing high-risk parts of the Hunua Ranges as a preventive measure.

Bogotá's culinary scene is diverse, featuring a mix of international cuisines alongside traditional Colombian fare. The city's gastronomic neighborhoods showcase a variety of restaurants offering European, American, and Middle Eastern dishes, with notable representations from countries such as Peru and Mexico in South America, and the United States in North America. Lebanese cuisine is also available, highlighting the city's multicultural dining options. A typical dish of Bogotá is ajiaco, a comforting chicken and potato soup made with three types of potatoes, corn on the cob, and guascas, a herb native to the region. This dish exemplifies the rich culinary heritage of Bogotá while reflecting the city's evolving food landscape.

The first impound races in NASCAR were implemented in 2005, with 21 of the 36 races in the NASCAR Nextel Cup Series run under the new rule. The concept, introduced by series director John Darby, aimed to discourage teams from spending excessive time and money on qualifying setups by impounding cars post-qualifying, requiring them to race with the same configuration. Darby hoped this would enhance competition by leveling the field. However, teams criticized the schedule changes for causing confusion and wasting time, leading NASCAR to reduce impound races to just five in 2006. Initially, the rule was intended to streamline race weekends and reduce costs, but backlash from drivers and teams prompted NASCAR to reassess. By 2007, only five races—both Richmond and Talladega events and the July Daytona race—remained as impound races. NASCAR president Mike Helton later acknowledged the rule "didn't fit the way we thought it would," reflecting the challenge of balancing competition and practicality.

During the 1990s, Fox River Grove experienced economic growth, leading to significant housing developments. In 1994, 6 out of 7 village trustees voted to approve a luxury housing development on 40 acres. This development was planned for Picnic Grove, which straddles the Fox River. The community reacted with mixed feelings, with some residents advocating for single-family homes instead. To address concerns, the Board of Trustees imposed requirements on developers to ensure the project aligned with community interests, including maintaining a drug-free environment and providing amenities that promote a high quality of life.

The feeder system, part of TransPeshawar's phase 2, comprises 8 routes covering 68 kilometers. Feeder buses use dedicated BRT lanes before exiting onto city streets. These routes add 100 new stops, with feeder stations spaced approximately 300-500 meters apart.

In his final years, Keeaumoku II suffered from a prolonged illness, which some chiefs initially attributed to sorcery. Seeking treatment, he was brought to Oahu, where foreign physicians attempted to aid him. During this time, missionary William Ellis visited him daily, offering spiritual guidance. The Grey Fantail (Rhipidura albiscapa) is a seasonal breeder, forming territories and engaging in mating behaviors typically observed from August onwards. These birds can raise multiple broods per season, with each brood consisting of 3-4 cream-colored eggs, often spotted with grey and brown. The incubation period lasts approximately two weeks, during which both parents share the responsibilities of incubating the eggs and feeding the chicks. Male grey fantails exhibit pronounced morphological changes during the breeding season, including large testes and pronounced cloacal protuberances, indicative of sperm competition. Interestingly, while males do not mate guard, they frequently attack their mates during nest-building attempts, suggesting a strategy to increase the likelihood of extra-pair copulations. Despite these aggressive behaviors, grey fantails show high levels of paternal care. However, nest depredation is a significant challenge, with 83% of clutches being depredated before fledging.

In a shared memory multiprocessor system with a separate cache memory for each processor, each processor has its own local cache. It is possible to have multiple copies of shared data: one in the main memory and one in the local cache of each requesting processor. When one of these copies is changed, the other copies must reflect this change. Cache coherence, the discipline ensuring timely propagation, guarantees that changes in the values of shared operands are propagated throughout the system.

The introduction of federal discrimination protections for LGBTI people under the Gillard Government came with assurances to religious groups that they would retain the ability to discriminate against individuals deemed inconsistent with their beliefs, as outlined in sections 37 and 38 of the *Sex Discrimination Act 1984*. Vladimir Matskevich served as the Soviet Agriculture Minister from 1955 to 1960, during the leadership of Nikita Khrushchev, and again from 1965 to 1973. Roger Jourdain was elected as the first tribal chairman of the Red Lake Band of Chippewa Indians in 1959. El Salvador boasts approximately 360 rivers forming 10 hydrographic regions. During the Mamluk period, multi-story buildings known as rab' (plural riba' or urbu) became common and continued to be a feature of housing in Cairo during the later Ottoman period. This type of housing was relatively unique to Cairo at the time, providing rental apartments that were often laid out as duplexes or triplexes. When attached to caravanserais, the two lower floors of these buildings were typically used for commercial purposes, such as warehouses or shops, while the upper floors served as accommodations for merchants and travelers. Notably, these apartment complexes had their own street entrances, separate from the commercial complex below. The oldest partially-preserved example of this architectural style is the Wikala of Amir Qawsun, built before 1341. Additionally, residential buildings in Cairo were organized into close-knit neighborhoods called harat, which often featured gates that could be closed off at night or during disturbances, enhancing the security and community aspect of urban living.

SAMOS 4, the first Program 101B satellite, was a U.S. Air Force first-generation photo surveillance satellite launched on 22 November 1961 at 00:00:00 GMT from Point Arguello, California, aboard an Atlas-Agena B rocket. Extensive pre-launch testing, including X-ray inspections for faulty transistors and super-cleaning of propellant tanks, was conducted to ensure mission success. However, at T+245 seconds, the Atlas booster experienced a guidance malfunction, resulting in a loss of pitch control. The booster jettisoned prematurely, and the sustainer engine burned to liquid oxygen depletion, causing the Agena stage to separate at an incorrect angle. As a result, the Agena's engine fired in the wrong direction, driving SAMOS 4 into the Pacific Ocean instead of achieving orbit. Despite these efforts, the mission ended in failure, marking another setback for the SAMOS program.

The colonial era of Rooty Hill began with its exploration in 1789 by Captain Watkin Tench, accompanied by surgeons Arndell and Lowes, two marines, and a convict. Historians were puzzled by the origin of the name "Rooty Hill" until a clue was found in early records of Norfolk Island, settled in March 1788 under Lieutenant Philip Gidley King. King noted the hillside's suitability for building Government House, which became known as "Mount Pitt." When King returned to New South Wales, he acquired over 4,000 acres near Rooty Hill. The name first appeared in print in the *Sydney Gazette* on 10 August 1811 and was later used for surveying Sydney's west. The connection to Norfolk Island's "rooty" terrain likely inspired the name.

The 1st Oklahoma Field Artillery Regiment was federally recognized on July 18, 1921, and consisted of a Regimental Headquarters and two firing battalions, including the White Horse Battery.

John Cleveley the Younger was the son of shipwright and artist John Cleveley and his twin brother Robert. Both brothers, like their father, worked in the Royal Dockyard at Deptford and received artistic training from him and Paul Sandby. They exhibited their works, combining topographical accuracy with architectural detail, at the Free Society of Artists and the Royal Academy, reaching a wider audience than their father.

During the off-season, the Vancouver Canucks signed defenceman Jason Garrison to a six-year contract valued at $27.6 million. Garrison was expected to fill the role of veteran defenceman Sami Salo, who left the Canucks after a decade to sign a two-year contract worth $7.5 million with the Tampa Bay Lightning. Following injuries to Kevin Bieksa, Garrison's performance improved significantly, as he became a key player on the Canucks' defense, logging substantial ice time and forming a strong partnership with Dan Hamhuis. Garrison's ability to adapt to playing out of position further highlighted his contributions during this period.

William Barnard Rhodes, described as one of the most successful opportunists of his age, returned to New Zealand in late 1839 hoping to secure title over vast amounts of land, in partnership with Daniel Cooper and James Holt. He acquired deeds to thousands of acres and established trading stations at carefully chosen landings. Choosing Wellington as his base, Rhodes built premises in Te Aro and by 1841 had completed a wharf and warehouses. He directed the family's land interests from Wellington, living at The Grange in Wadestown. By 1843, Rhodes had bought out Cooper and Holt's share of the business.

Werner & Mertz has adopted a market position of corporate social responsibility using its brand names Frosch and green care PROFESSIONAL. In 1986, the company introduced the first phosphate-free household cleaning agent under the brand name Frosch. Werner & Mertz's brands Frosch, Erdal, tana PROFESSIONAL, green care PROFESSIONAL, Rorax, Emsal, and Bionicdry offer hygiene and cleaning products. The Erdal shoe care range has been solvent-free since 2009.

**Comments**  
In an August 17, 1991 article, U.S. District Judge Robert G. Doumar stated that HIV-positive individuals who engage in unprotected sex "should be shot." In 2017, while presiding over a child pornography case, he condemned producers as "despicable." Doumar confirmed these remarks in an interview with *The Virginian-Pilot*. His judicial conduct has drawn criticism, including reversal by the 4th Circuit in a transgender rights case.

At the outset of the COVID-19 pandemic, experts speculated that misinformation could induce psychiatric symptoms like fear and anxiety in healthy individuals, worsen pre-existing mental health conditions, and trigger panic attacks and phobias. Often referred to as "Disease X," COVID-19 became the focus of intense scientific scrutiny. Virologist Shi Zhengli, Director of the Center for Emerging Infectious Diseases of the Wuhan Institute of Virology, studies bat-borne viruses. Marion Koopmans, director of the WHO-cooperation center for Emerging Diseases at the Erasmus MC and scientific director of Emerging Diseases of the Netherlands Centre for One Health, is also a member of the WHO team. Peter Daszak, president of EcoHealth Alliance, is primarily concerned with the animal origins of the virus and is also a member of the WHO team.

KTEN clears the entire schedule of the NBC network, airing some of its programs at different times than most affiliates. KTEN broadcasts the soap opera Days of Our Lives at 12 PM, while the majority of NBC stations air it at 1 PM. Additionally, KTEN delays NBC's weekday overnight lineup by one hour, airing Last Call with Carson Daly at 12:38 AM and following it with Today at 2:07 AM. The weekend schedule for the overnight block is also delayed by one hour. As of September 2017, KTEN featured syndicated programs such as Rachael Ray and Inside Edition.

Until the 1940s, some schools in the Suffern Central School District were racially segregated. In 1943, the Brook School, later known as the Hillburn School, was desegregated under the legal advocacy of Thurgood Marshall, who would later become a U.S. Supreme Court Justice.

Patrick Abram Seale was born in Belfast on May 7, 1930, to Morris Seale, a respected Arabist and biblical scholar who served as an Irish Presbyterian missionary in Syria. He spent most of his first 14 years in Syria, where he developed a deep connection to the region. Seale attended Balliol and St. Anthony's College at Oxford, specializing in Middle Eastern history, and obtained a degree in this field. His sister, Reine Attal, was a midwife.

*Arundo donax*, commonly known as giant reed, is a highly invasive plant in southwestern North American rivers. Environmental scientists and land managers are concerned about its promotion as a biofuel in other regions. Introduced from the Mediterranean to California in the 1820s, it was initially used for roofing material and erosion control in drainage canals in the Los Angeles area. Through invasive spread and subsequent plantings as an ornamental plant, it has become naturalised throughout warm coastal freshwaters of North America.

Germaine Williams, born on December 9, 1974, in Kingston, Jamaica, is a Jamaican rapper known as Canibus. He is of West Indian descent. His father, Basil Williams, was a Jamaican and West Indian cricketer. Due to his mother's career requiring frequent relocation, the family lived in various locations, including The Bronx, Newark, New Jersey, Washington, D.C., Atlanta, Miami, Buffalo, and London. Canibus has stated that he was an introverted child. After completing high school in 1992, he worked for AT&T Corporation for a year. He later pursued his interest in computers by studying computer science at DeKalb College in Atlanta.

Nadezhda Plevitskaya (born Nadezhda Vinnikova) was born in 1884 in the village of Vinnikovo near Kursk to a peasant family. Her love for singing, inherited from her parents, led her to join the church choir of the Trinity Monastery in Kursk, where she stayed as a novice for over two years. After leaving the monastery, she moved to Kiev, where she became a professional singer and married Edmund Plewicki. The couple later moved to Moscow, where Plevitskaya began performing at the renowned Yar Restaurant. Her breakthrough came in 1909 at the Nizhny Novgorod Fair, where her performance impressed the famous tenor Leonid Sobinov, who invited her to sing at a charity concert, launching her career.

Destruction of the olfactory tract leads to ipsilateral anosmia, where smell loss affects the same side as the damage. Kallmann syndrome, a rare hormonal disorder, often presents with anosmia. Congenital anosmia is indicated by the absence or underdevelopment of the olfactory sulci and reduced olfactory bulb volume.

Margaret Louise Cuninggim was born in Nashville, Tennessee. Her father, Jesse Lee Cuninggim, was a Methodist clergyman and the President of Scarritt College for Christian Workers in Nashville. Her brother, Merrimon Cuninggim (1911-1995), was also a Methodist clergyman, and served as the President of Salem College in Winston-Salem, North Carolina. She graduated from Duke University in Durham, North Carolina in 1936 and went on to earn a master's degree from Columbia University in New York City in 1947 and a Doctorate of Education from Northwestern University in Evanston, Illinois in 1958.

Rentenbank’s special promotional loans are geared towards businesses in the agricultural, horticultural, viticultural and silvicultural sectors, including manufacturers of agricultural input materials as well as agriculture-related trade and service companies. The bank also finances projects in the food industry and the associated upstream and downstream industries. Further, Rentenbank promotes civic engagement and public-sector investments in rural areas. The bank’s statutory promotional mandate explicitly includes promoting renewable energy, renewable agricultural resources, organic farming, agriculture-related environmental and consumer protection, and animal welfare. The loans are extended via local banks on a competitively neutral basis. In addition, Rentenbank awards grants for innovations and applied research projects, as well as for projects and institutions that are of particular importance to agribusiness and rural areas. Rentenbank’s Rehwinkel Foundation supports scientific research, promotes events that foster collaboration between agricultural science and industry, and awards scholarships to students on relevant Master’s programmes. Rentenbank focuses its efforts on promoting innovation, in particular start-ups that are developing new, tech-based business ideas that can play a vital role in driving innovation in agriculture.

Spiro earned dual bachelor's degrees in Political Science and Communication Arts from the University of Wisconsin–Madison. He then attended the University of Texas at Austin, where he received a master's degree in Film Production. His early work includes film adaptations of The Convict, a short story by James Lee Burke, and The Suicide Club, a story by Robert Louis Stevenson, which starred Jonathan Pryce and Paul Bettany.

In December 2018, it was reported that the airline had suffered heavy losses in recent years, especially during 2018 and that its owners were seeking potential buyers for the airline. On 31 January 2019, it was reported that payment of employee salaries would be delayed.
